title: Barcelona captain’s relationship with club deteriorates after serious World Cup injury – report
description: According to Catalunya Radio, relations between FC Barcelona and Frenkie de Jong have deteriorated significantly following the midfielder’s return from the FIFA World Cup with a severe knee injury. ...
fullText:
According to Catalunya Radio, relations between FC Barcelona and Frenkie de Jong have deteriorated significantly following the midfielder’s return from the FIFA World Cup with a severe knee injury.

Internal friction has mounted inside the Catalan club regarding how the physical setback occurred and how it was subsequently managed by the player and the Netherlands national team during the tournament.

The club officials are understood to be very angry and frustrated about the circumstances surrounding the player’s medical treatment while away on international duty.

Initial medical assessments conducted at the Ciutat Esportiva Joan Gamper indicate that the Dutch international will be sidelined for a period ranging between three to four months.

Out for 3-4 months. (Photo by Mike Hewitt/Getty Images)

While more tests are scheduled to take place in the coming days, early evaluations of De Jong’s right knee revealed significant instability and potential ligament damage, completely disrupting head coach Hansi Flick’s tactical planning ahead of the new season.

The club’s hierarchy believes the injury was severely exacerbated because the player pushed through intense discomfort to participate in the knockout rounds.

De Jong featured in pivotal matches while utilizing heavy bandaging and receiving painkilling injections, a medical approach that Barcelona’s staff feel compromised his long-term physical stability.

It is understandable that Barça are not at all happy with the developments, considering that De Jong already missed a chunk of matches last season due to injury and fitness issues.

De Jong’s injury has disrupted Flick’s plans. (Photo by Eric Alonso/Getty Images)

As per the report, a definitive decision regarding the exact clinical treatment pathway will be finalized on Friday, following a final round of specialised medical tests.

While the player’s camp has traditionally favoured conservative, non-invasive recovery methods to avoid the operating table, Barcelona’s institutional stance is clear.

The club is strongly pushing for immediate surgery to definitively resolve the underlying chronic instability in the knee joint and to ensure full healing and prevent subsequent relapses, even if it delays his competitive re-integration into the first-team squad until late autumn.

It remains to be seen what De Jong decides, with an official statement regarding his injury and recovery likely to arrive once the player chooses his next move.

title: Argentina's Paredes should not be suspended for confrontation - Spain's Gavi
description: Spain midfielder Gavi does not think Argentina's Leandro Paredes should be suspended for his part in the fracas after the World Cup final.
fullText:
Spain midfielder Gavi does not believe Argentina's Leandro Paredes should be suspended for his part in the confrontation following the World Cup final.

Argentina players Paredes, Nahuel Molina and Thiago Almada plus assistant coach Roberto Ayala all appeared to be involved in the fracas on the pitch as Spain celebrated their 1-0 win.

Paredes seemed to push Spain's Eric Garcia by the throat and unused substitute Gavi tried to intervene but was forced to the ground by Almada.

Paredes pushed the Barcelona midfielder to the turf by putting his hand in the Spaniard's face.

Fifa has appointed a disciplinary and ethics prosecutor to assess what happened and any player or coach involved could receive a suspension while the Argentine Football Association could also be fined.

"I don't think [Paredes] should be suspended," Gavi told El Partidazo de COPE, external.

"I understand it's not a good image for children but I think there's also that side to football, which is a bit more physical, more aggressive.

"The most logical thing would have been to send him off during the match and that's it. In the end, I think it's all football and it has to be that way."

Fifa opens investigation into Argentina post-final trouble

Fernandez defends Argentina as Messi questions remain

Ayala said he would apologise to Spain's Dani Olmo after he appeared to strike the Barcelona midfielder in the face - which he says was "more of a push than a punch".

"When the match ended, I saw a scuffle in the middle of the pitch and I went over to stop it from escalating, because that's not who we are," Ayala told Capital Radio in Valencia.

"I take responsibility for what I did. My intention was simply to go and separate them, nothing more."

It was incorrectly reported on Fifa's commentator information system that Paredes had been sent off by referee Slavko Vincic for pushing Garcia by the throat.

Paredes' red card was deleted on the system shortly afterwards and Fifa has confirmed to BBC Sport that no disciplinary action was taken at the time.

Argentina's Enzo Fernandez was dismissed during the final, receiving a second yellow card in second-half added time.

title: FIFA World Cup breaks 32-year Fox and Telemundo record
description: Soccer is perhaps the most popular sport in the world, even if it is not the biggest in the United States of America. The NFL, NBA, etc, are generally more popular than ‘football’, but the 2026 World Cup meant a lot more eyes were on soccer across America this summer.
fullText:
Soccer is perhaps the most popular sport in the world, even if it is not the biggest in the United States of America.

The NFL, NBA, etc, are generally more popular than ‘football’, but the 2026 World Cup meant a lot more eyes were on soccer across America this summer.

In turn, it is perhaps not surprising to see the tournament breaking viewership records on Fox and Telemundo.

The USMNT were knocked out of the 2026 World Cup in the Round of 16 after losing 4-1 to Belgium.

Despite the early exit, it seems like many were still heavily invested in the competition all the way up to the final.

This was made clear after Fox’s Michael Mulvihill tweeted that the final between Spain and Argentina set viewership records on the network.

He stated: “The World Cup Final averaged 66.4 million viewers in the U.S. on FOX and Telemundo combined. 42.5 million on FOX. 23.9 million on Telemundo.

“Other than Super Bowls, this is the largest sports audience since the 1994 Winter Olympics.”

Soccer in America still has some way to go if it is to reach the heights of the Super Bowl, however.

The 2026 showpiece is reported to have had over 125 million viewers on Fox earlier this year, around double that of the World Cup.

Of course, that may have been different had the USMNT reached the final, but that was always going to be an unlikely scenario.

Still, it may lead to more people inside the USA starting to take soccer a bit more seriously, given there is clearly a decent audience to capitalise on.

title: NBC earns best Open Championship audience since 2022
description: American sports fans looking for an appetizer prior to Sunday’s World Cup final seemed to get one in the 154th Open Championship. NBC’s final-round coverage of the last men’s golf major of the year averaged 4.3 million viewers, up six percent from last year’s victory from Scottie Scheffler at Royal Portrush (4.1 million viewers). Ryan…
fullText:
American sports fans looking for an appetizer prior to Sunday’s World Cup final seemed to get one in the 154th Open Championship.

NBC’s final-round coverage of the last men’s golf major of the year averaged 4.3 million viewers, up six percent from last year’s victory from Scottie Scheffler at Royal Portrush (4.1 million viewers). Ryan Fox’s dramatic win capped with a birdie on the 72nd hole peaked at 6.1 million viewers during the 1:15 p.m. ET quarter-hour.

As usual, the Open Championship was the least-watched of the four majors due to a less favorable time zone for the American audience. NBC’s final-round window began at 9 a.m. and went through 2 p.m. ET, whereas final-round coverage of the Masters, for instance, begins at 2 p.m. and typically runs until about 7 p.m. ET. Those Sunday afternoon hours are prime viewing times for the U.S. audience. Sunday morning? Not so much.

As Sports Business Journal’s Josh Carpenter lays out, June’s U.S. Open averaged 5.5 million viewers in the final round, the PGA Championship 5.7 million viewers in May, and the Masters 13.9 million viewers in April.

NBC: 4.3 million viewers for the final round of @TheOpen, its best since 2022, though with the addition of Big Data, fair to wonder if last year (4.1M) would have outdrawn it.

Major viewership this year (final rounds)

Masters: 13.9M
 PGA: 5.7M
 U.S. Open: 5.5M
 Open: 4.3M pic.twitter.com/1jvOdTBxTz

— Josh Carpenter (@JoshACarpenter) July 21, 2026

Still, holding an audience of 4.3 million viewers across a five-hour window early on Sunday is a solid result for NBC. It should be noted, this is the first Open Championship to be measured using Nielsen’s new Big Data + Panel standard, and the six percent year-over-year increase falls within the range that could solely be explained by the changes.

Saturday saw a larger year-over-year bump, however. Third-round coverage on NBC and Peacock averaged 3.7 million viewers, up 16 percent versus last year (3.2 million viewers) and good for the most-watched third round since 2018.

title: Posts falsely claim Lamine Yamal snubbed Donald Trump after World Cup win
description: Spain star Lamine Yamal did not avoid shaking Donald Trump's hand after the World Cup final, contrary to posts claiming the teenager snubbed the US president and speculating he may have done so because of US policy regarding Palestinians. The posts cited as proof a video of the interaction filmed from behind Yamal that partially obscures Trump, but AFP photos and footage of the post-match ceremony show the pair did shake hands.
fullText:
Spain star Lamine Yamal did not avoid shaking Donald Trump's hand after the World Cup final, contrary to posts claiming the teenager snubbed the US president and speculating he may have done so because of US policy regarding Palestinians. The posts cited as proof a video of the interaction filmed from behind Yamal that partially obscures Trump, but AFP photos and footage of the post-match ceremony show the pair did shake hands.

"Lamine Yamal did not shake hands with Trump; he avoided the US president," says the Bengali-language caption of a YouTube video shared on July 20, 2026.

The video, which has been viewed more than 334,000 times, appears to show Spain's 19-year-old winger Lamine Yamal going to collect his World Cup winner's medal. He is first met by FIFA head Gianni Infantino before briefly stopping in front of US President Donald Trump, who drapes the medal over Yamal's head.

According to the video's Bengali-language voiceover, Yamal did not shake Trump's hand. It speculates this may be because of Trump's "foreign policy towards Palestinians".

The clip was also shared in similar YouTube and Facebook posts after Spain's 1-0 win over Argentina in the World Cup final on July 19, with the US president then presenting the World Cup trophy to La Roja ( archived link ).

"Lamine Yamal did the right thing 100 percent," says a comment on one of the posts, while another says "Thanks Spain, and thanks Yamal."

There is strong support for the Palestinian people in Muslim-majority Bangladesh, and the government reaffirmed its "steadfast commitment to the legitimate rights of the Palestinian people and the establishment of an independent and sovereign State of Palestine" in July ( archived link ).

Yamal has also expressed support for the Palestinian cause in the past, most recently during his club side Barcelona's title celebrations in May when he waved a Palestinian flag during an open-top bus parade ( archived link ).

Trump, meanwhile, has firmly backed Israel despite growing international outrage over its offensive in the Palestinian territory of Gaza, in response to the October 7, 2023 attacks carried out by Gaza-based militants Hamas ( archived link ).

There was an international  backlash when Trump said he wanted to take control of the Gaza strip and "permanently" resettle Palestinians in other countries ( archived link ).

The US president's Gaza peace plan -- which was endorsed by the UN Security Council in November 2025 -- does, however, include a path to a future Palestinian state ( archived link ).

The claims circulating on social media that Yamal avoided shaking Trump's hand are false, with the angle the falsely shared video appearing to have obscured the handshake.

Several  AFP photos , taken from a different angle to the falsely shared video, show  Yamal and Trump shaking hands as the teenager received his winner's medal.

Moreover, a  keyword search on Google led to similar footage shared on the verified YouTube channels of the White House and Fox Sports showing the pair shaking hands (archived  here and here ).

title: 'Thank you for all the unforgettable memories' - Anderson says goodbye
description: Elliot Anderson has posted an emotional goodbye to Nottingham Forest as he prepares to join Manchester City.  Earlier in July, City confirmed an agreement has been reached to sign Anderson, stating the "formalities of the move will be finalised upon his return to England" after the World Cup.  BBC Sport reported a deal had been put in place on 25 June.
fullText:
Elliot Anderson has posted an emotional goodbye to Nottingham Forest as he prepares to join Manchester City.

Earlier in July, City confirmed an agreement has been reached to sign Anderson, stating the "formalities of the move will be finalised upon his return to England" after the World Cup. BBC Sport reported a deal had been put in place on 25 June.

The 23-year-old made 92 appearances across two seasons at Forest after joining from boyhood club Newcastle in 2024, helping them qualify for Europe in 2024-25 and reach the Europa League semi-finals the following year.

The goodbye message posted by Anderson on social media read: "Forest family,

"Firstly I am so grateful for the love and support you have shown me over the last 2 years especially towards the end of this season. From the minute I walked through the door I fell in love with this club.

"I want to thank Mr Marinakas, the staff and all players for welcoming me and making my time here so enjoyable, this club gave me the opportunity to grow as a player and a person.

"Bringing the club European football again after 29 years is what all of you fans deserved and to be a helping hand in that is so special.

"Nottingham forest will always have a special place in my heart. Thank you for all the unforgettable memories."

title: Real Madrid schedule first preseason match under Jose Mourinho
description: Jose Mourinho has been training the available Real Madrid players for some days now, and even the first player who participated in the World Cup, Arda Guler, has also joined up with him at Valdebebas....
fullText:
Jose Mourinho has been training the available Real Madrid players for some days now, and even the first player who participated in the World Cup, Arda Guler, has also joined up with him at Valdebebas. The new manager has also called up several youth team players to train under him as well.

After Guler’s return, several other players are expected to join the Portuguese trainer in the coming few days. Federico Valverde, whose Uruguay side was also eliminated in the group stage, like Guler’s Turkiye, will be coming back soon, as will be Antonio Rudiger, whose German side was eliminated in the Round of 32.

However, it remains to be seen whether they will be ready and available for the first training match of the preseason, which is scheduled in only a few days’ time. As MARCA has reported, Jose Mourinho will play his first match with Real Madrid against Alcorcon, this Friday, 24 July.

This is going to be a training match, and not a friendly, and thus, will be played behind closed doors. Unlimited substitutions will be allowed, as this will be the first training match after the holidays for players, and the managers will also want to test several of their players and setups.

Alcorcon are also looking to debut in their new season kits, which are expected to be unveiled on Thursday, in this training match against Real Madrid. As is the case with Jose Mourinho, the Alcorcon manager, Fran Justo, will also be overseeing this game as the first of his stint on his team’s bench.

Although it is not going to be a competitive game, it will provide a good opportunity for several Real Madrid youngsters to showcase their talents to Jose Mourinho, and thus make a place for themselves in the coach’s plans for the upcoming season.

title: Las Vegas Raiders owner Mark Davis weighs in on grass fields movement after the World Cup
description: Las Vegas Raiders owner Mark Davis has broken his silence on the demand for NFL teams to use grass fields on the back of this summer’s World Cup. The Allegiant Stadium was not one of the host venues for the United States at the World Cup, but it is one of the NFL stadiums that uses a natural grass playing surface.
fullText:
Las Vegas Raiders owner Mark Davis has broken his silence on the demand for NFL teams to use grass fields on the back of this summer’s World Cup.

The Allegiant Stadium was not one of the host venues for the United States at the World Cup, but it is one of the NFL stadiums that uses a natural grass playing surface.

There is an argument to be made that a natural surface is easier for Vegas to maintain given it is an indoor venue, which does not expose the grass to the elements.

However, Davis is one of the owners who believes playing on a grass field is largely beneficial for his players’ health and wellbeing.

This is not a view that is shared across the league, with numerous NFL players now protesting their thoughts after seeing grass pitches laid down for the World Cup soccer teams.

As the Dallas Cowboys have already ripped up their grass field at AT&T Stadium, Davis has issued a message on the importance of his players playing on a natural surface.

In recent days, Seattle Seahawks fans have urged their new owners to lay down a grass surface at Lumen Field.

It is proving the hottest topic of discussion across the league, as the players make their voices heard over social media.

“There was no way I was going to build the stadium or anything else to play on artificial turf,” said the 71-year-old.

“I just always felt that the safety issue, number one, and I grew up around football and the grass and mud and the smell of those uniforms after the game.”

The Raiders’ stadium cost just shy of $2 billion to build and Davis did not cost cut by going with an artificial playing surface for his players.

Pat McAfee has backed the NFL players’ protest for grass fields, with Davis also backing the notion it should be an investment from the owners towards their players’ safety.

Owners across the league are happy to pay out huge wages to their star players, but there is a clear hesitation from some of them to splash out on installing and maintaining a grass field.

Witnessing the same stadiums across the nation suddenly install grass for the World Cup will have come as a slap in the face for NFL players across the league.

This is why the movement is picking up steam ahead of the 2026 season getting underway.

title: Cristiano Ronaldo Ranks Fourth In Post-World Cup Instagram Gains, Adding 11.4M Followers
description: Cristiano Ronaldo emerged as one of the biggest social media winners of the 2026 FIFA World Cup, finishing fourth among The Cristiano Ronaldo Ranks Fourth In Post-World Cup Instagram Gains, Adding 11.4M Followers first appeared on The Blast
fullText:
Cristiano Ronaldo emerged as one of the biggest social media winners of the 2026 FIFA World Cup, finishing fourth among the tournament’s top Instagram gainers. The Portugal star added 11.4 million followers despite his country failing to reach the latter stages.

Ronaldo also sparked controversy after his verified account appeared to endorse a post accusing FIFA of favoring Argentina at the competition.

Overall, Norway striker Erling Haaland led all other players at the World Cup in growth of IG numbers, as he added a whopping 32.1 million followers to his audience.

Cristiano Ronaldo may already be the most-followed athlete on Instagram, but the Portuguese icon still proved the FIFA World Cup could grow even the biggest global brands.

At the just-concluded global soccer showpiece, Ronaldo finished fourth among the tournament’s biggest Instagram gainers. According to a Sportico analysis of social media growth during the World Cup, the five-time Ballon d’Or winner added 11.4 million followers during the 38-day competition to climb from 665.6 million to a staggering 677 million.

This boost represented a relatively modest 1.71% increase compared to that of the tournament’s breakout stars, but it was still enough to place Ronaldo behind only Erling Haaland, Vozinha, and Jude Bellingham in total followers gained after the tournament.

The continued social media dominance by the Al-Nassr forward stands out even more when you consider Portugal failed to reach the latter stages.

Ronaldo may have added another 11.4 million followers, but he was comfortably outpaced by Haaland, who topped the rankings after gaining 32.1 million during the tournament.

The Norway striker’s following reportedly surged from 40.6 million to 72.7 million, representing an increase of roughly 80%. His quirky and playful posts also helped introduce his personality to a wider American audience as Norway reached the quarterfinals.

Days after being knocked out of the tournament by England, Haaland was spotted partying with Michele Morrone at a Dolce & Gabbana event in Italy, as reported by The Blast.

On the pitch, Haaland finished the tournament with seven goals, helping Norway reach the quarterfinals in their first World Cup appearance since 1998.

The towering forward also won over fans across the United States with his personality and off-field adventures. He attended Game 5 of the NHL Stanley Cup Final in North Carolina before Norway’s tournament opener, cheered on the Carolina Hurricanes, and explored local attractions during breaks in the schedule.

Haaland even went viral after buying a taxidermied raccoon from a Texas souvenir shop and joking on social media that “It followed me home.” Those memorable moments, combined with his performances for Norway, would have undoubtedly helped him on his way to becoming the tournament’s biggest Instagram winner.

Cape Verde goalkeeper Vozinha was arguably the list’s biggest surprise, finishing second with 29.4 million new followers after beginning the tournament with an audience of only around 50,000. England star Jude Bellingham placed third with 12 million, narrowly ahead of Ronaldo.

Spain sensation Lamine Yamal followed Ronaldo in fifth place after gaining 11 million followers, while Neymar and Lionel Messi added 7.7 million and 7.5 million, respectively. Mexico youngster Gilberto Mora, France’s Michael Olise, and Brazilian forward Endrick completed the top 10 with gains ranging from 5.6 million to 6.6 million.

One particularly notable omission was Kylian Mbappé, who failed to crack the top 10 despite France’s run to the semifinals. Brazil, meanwhile, was the only country to place two players on the list, with Neymar and Endrick both enjoying sizeable boosts.

Ronaldo also found himself at the center of an off-field controversy after fans noticed his verified Instagram account had liked a video alleging FIFA helped Lionel Messi’s Argentina reach the 2026 World Cup final.

The post, which featured comments from Spanish journalist Pilar Rodríguez Losantos accusing football’s governing body of favoring the defending champions, quickly went viral and reignited debate over Argentina’s run to the title match.

Screenshots of the would-be like spread rapidly across social media before it later disappeared, though the Portuguese superstar has not publicly addressed the incident or clarified whether the interaction was intentional.

title: Rebecca Lowe details how helicopter ride nearly derailed entire Fox World Cup broadcast
description: Those who tuned in for Fox’s four-hour World Cup final pregame show on Sunday witnessed Rebecca Lowe and the rest of the network’s studio crew travel by land, air, and sea to make it from Brooklyn Bridge Park to MetLife Stadium in time for kickoff. What viewers might not have realized is how the intricate…
fullText:
Those who tuned in for Fox’s four-hour World Cup final pregame show on Sunday witnessed Rebecca Lowe and the rest of the network’s studio crew travel by land, air, and sea to make it from Brooklyn Bridge Park to MetLife Stadium in time for kickoff. What viewers might not have realized is how the intricate plan to transport the crew almost went entirely wrong.

Fox began coverage of the World Cup final with Lowe, Thierry Henry, Zlatan Ibrahimović, and Alexi Lalas live from a set located in Brooklyn Bridge Park, providing a stunning backdrop for the network’s 2026 World Cup swan song. Midway through the show, the crew would leave the set, take a ferry across the East River, drive to a helipad, and chopper to another helipad located near MetLife Stadium, where Spain and Argentina would kick off at 3 p.m. ET.

That itinerary did not go as planned.

Lowe, appearing on the After The Whistle podcast, explained how she and her colleagues got massively delayed, causing them to wonder if they’d even make it to the final at all.

“We had to get from Brooklyn Bridge Park all the way to MetLife while we were on air. So we started with a boat from the ferry stop over the East River to another pier, into a car to take us a couple of piers down to the heliport,” Lowe explained. “Now, I’ve been on a helicopter once, and I swore I’d never go on one again, but then Fox Sports employed me for the World Cup, and I said, ‘Sure, that sounds great.’

“So, we get in the helicopter. It’s supposed to be seven minutes. Wasn’t seven minutes, it was 30 minutes. And let’s just say, Zlatan, God bless him, didn’t say a single word. I was pretty close to that as well, pretty scared, not going to lie. We were circling. I kept seeing the Yankee Stadium again and again and again. …Terrifying, and it’s very bumpy up there, and it was horrible. Alexi Lalas, I’ve never seen him happier. Out the window, pointing out all the amazing sights and sounds, I was like, ‘This guy.’

“We eventually land at the wrong helipad. So now we’re at a random- by the way, we had to be back on air at 1:15. We eventually get out, we film our little link, and eventually realize we’re not at Teterboro, I think is it called? We’re in Kearny, New Jersey. We didn’t know where we were. We were supposed to meet a police escort, a couple of cars, to take us then to MetLife. Well, they were at Teterboro. So now we’re calling them, they come to get us, that’s 15 minutes. We’re filming another piece just saying, ‘We don’t know where we are.’

“And at this point, I’m thinking, ‘Are we going to make it?’ And at this point, we’re about an hour and a half before kickoff of a World Cup final, and I’m thinking, ‘I’ve done this World Cup for 39 days, and I’m not going to get to do the final because I’m going to be in a car on the New Jersey Turnpike.’

“So at this point … we have to be on air for 1:54. So we get into the police escort, we were going 105 miles an hour. 105. And it was very scary. So that was the helicopter — scary. Then we had the police escort — scary. Then we finally get there, we circle the stadium a couple times because no one knows how to get into this place. Pull up, we go under, we get on the elevator, it’s now 1:51. I go on the set at 1:52. We go on air at 1:54.”

It seems like Murphy’s Law hit the Fox Sports World Cup studio at the worst possible time. Luckily, the team was able to make it to the stadium about an hour before the match started, and the only collateral damage from the entire affair was missing an interview with Timothée Chalamet. Oh, well. That’ll just have to wait until 2030.

The post Rebecca Lowe details how helicopter ride nearly derailed entire Fox World Cup broadcast appeared first on Awful Announcing.

title: From self-doubt to Spanish football folklore: Ferran Torres’ journey to the top
description: The clock read 106 minutes when the ball finally finds Ferran Torres.For most of the evening in New Jersey, Spain have pushed and probed against an Argentina side determined to drag the World Cup fina...
fullText:
The clock read 106 minutes when the ball finally finds Ferran Torres.

For most of the evening in New Jersey, Spain have pushed and probed against an Argentina side determined to drag the World Cup final towards penalties.

20 attempts have come and gone. Lionel Messi has been suffocated, Lamine Yamal subdued, and the biggest match in football has slowly tightened into a knot nobody seems capable of undoing.

Then, two of Spain’s substitutes change the narrative of the game. Nico Williams and Ferran combine. The Barcelona forward does what he does best and makes the perfect run into the box.

He takes the ball on the half-volley and puts his left foot through it with the perfect striker’s instinct. A split second later, the net bulges.

In an instant, Ferran is no longer simply a Barcelona forward, a useful squad player and a footballer who has spent years being discussed in terms of what he is not.

He is the man who has scored the goal that makes Spain champions of the world.

The man of the moment. (Photo by David Ramos/Getty Images)

The goal took seconds. The journey towards it took him years.

Because long before Ferran stood at the summit of football, he already knew what it felt like to be somewhere close to the bottom.

Ferran arrived at Barcelona from Manchester City in the winter of 2021, signing a contract until 2027 and stepping into a storied club who were going through a very difficult period.

Barcelona were rebuilding financially, institutionally and competitively. Ferran, still only 21, was expected to become part of the solution almost immediately.

There were good nights, goals, flashes of the intelligent runner Spain had already come to trust.

However, Barça have always been a difficult club to be at. A missed chance does not disappear when the final whistle blows; it gets blown out of proportion.

Another miss arrives and joins the first. And then another. Soon, a striker is no longer simply trying to score. He is trying to escape without becoming the subject of constant criticism yet again.

After Barcelona’s Europa League elimination by Eintracht Frankfurt the previous season, he said he had fallen into what he described as a ‘bottomless pit’ and did not know how to get out.

“I felt that I had fallen into a bottomless pit. I had never collapsed so much.”

His confidence had disappeared. Everything was affecting him. Eventually, he made the decision to work with a psychologist.

The road to the top has not been easy for Ferran. (Photo by David Ramos/Getty Images)

Perhaps the most revealing part of his admission, though, was what football had become inside his own head.

Ferran explained that he had become too obsessed with scoring. The goal was no longer the consequence of playing well; it had become the measurement of whether he was worth anything at all.

His change began when he learned to enjoy playing again rather than treating every match as another referendum on himself.

Because it would be easy, after New Jersey, to turn Ferran’s story into something simplistic.

The footballer struggles, seeks help, scores a World Cup-winning goal, and everything is suddenly complete.

Mental health does not work like that. A goal does not cure anxiety. A trophy does not erase difficult years.

The World Cup was not Ferran’s victorious moment. It came much earlier, when he admitted that he was struggling.

Football always notices the moment when the transformation becomes visible. Unfortunately, it does not care for the hundreds of days of work behind it.

For Ferran, there was no sudden reinvention. There were training sessions. Substitute appearances. Runs that were not found.

There were chances that were missed and chances that were taken. There were matches on the wing, matches through the centre, games in which he started and others in which his job was to wait.

Slowly, something changed. The obsession with being indispensable gave way to the value of being useful. The “Shark mentality” came forth and helped him rediscover his form and self-belief.

“I am very confident, trying to give my best version in everything that can help. Simply every time I’m on the field, it doesn’t matter if I am a starter or a substitute, I try to give my best and help.”

And then ‘The Shark’ came to life. (Photo by David Ramos/Getty Images)

That became one of Ferran’s greatest strengths under Hansi Flick. By May 2025, he had scored 19 goals and supplied seven assists for Barcelona despite starting only 19 of his 45 appearances to that point.

His 26 direct goal contributions had come at a rate of one every 73 minutes. He was producing without demanding the team revolve around him. That was when another final came along.

Barcelona were losing 2-1 to Real Madrid in the 2025 Copa del Rey decider when Ferran equalised with six minutes remaining, dragging the match into extra time before Jules Kounde eventually won it in the 116th minute.

Ferran finished the competition as its six-goal top scorer and was named player of the match in the final.

The pattern was beginning to form. Not the pattern of a superstar demanding centre stage, but something subtler.

When the game became uncomfortable, Ferran stayed available. He was rarely the glamorous solution but always the practical one.

The following season brought further proof. Ferran scored 16 league goals as Barcelona won La Liga, sharing the Zarra Trophy with Lamine Yamal as the competition’s highest-scoring Spanish player.

He had done so without taking any penalties through the course of the season, unlike his younger counterpart.

It was the first time a Barcelona player had won the award while representing the club, and it was fitting that Ferran was among those to achieve that feat.

Even the 2026 World Cup was not smooth sailing for the Spaniard. It asked Ferran to prove something else: patience.

His only start came in the opening game against Cape Verde, where he had La Roja’s best chance but hit the cross bar from close range in the 0-0 draw.

He then found the back of the net against Saudi Arabia coming off the bench, only for the goal to be chalked off for a marginal offside.

As Ferran waited for VAR’s decision on whether or not the goal would stand, the cameras caught him saying to himself, “Please, let it be a goal. Please…”.

It offered a small glimpse into just how much Ferran wanted to get off the mark at the tournament. Then came his first note of contribution.

In the Round of 16 against Portugal, with the match poised and elimination looming for the team that blinked first, Ferran threaded the pass that allowed Mikel Merino to score the 91st-minute winner.

The Arsenal man hogged all the limelight, and rightly so, and Ferran’s contribution was lost in the background yet again.

Spain’s tournament did not belong to him. This is not the story of one player carrying a nation from the first game to the last.

That is precisely why the ending fits.

Perhaps an earlier version of him would have viewed the substitutes’ bench as a judgement. A statement about status.

However, the Ferran at MetLife Stadium is no longer about that. He waits for the minutes he is given. He tries to make the most of them. This time, he succeeds.

The moment Ferran etched his name into the history books. (Photo by Al Bello/Getty Images)

It is the sort of moment that football retrospectively makes look inevitable.

Of course it had to be him. Of course it was the player who had spent years learning that he could not control when chances came, only whether he was ready when they did.

Of course, it had to be the player who was seen talking to himself earlier in the tournament praying that a goal was given.

Afterwards, Ferran rejected the idea that the goal belonged only to him. He said:

“I think the goal is the least important thing. At the end of the day, the goal belongs to 47 million people, not to me.”

However, history will be less democratic. The goal will belong to him. History will remember his name whenever it references Spain’s second star.

There is one comparison that cannot be entirely avoided.

In 2010, another Barcelona player stepped into extra time in a World Cup final and altered Spanish football forever.

Andres Iniesta scored against the Netherlands in the 116th minute to deliver Spain’s first world title.

Sixteen years later, Ferran scored in the 106th to deliver the second.

The comparison is obvious, but the similarities should probably end there.

They are different players, from different teams, carrying different stories. Iniesta’s place in Spanish football was already immense before Johannesburg. Ferran’s was not.

The folklore will always reference the patterns, though.

Spain now have two World Cup-winning goals. Both were scored in extra time. Both were scored by Barcelona footballers.

Ten minutes apart on the clock. Sixteen years apart in history.

A day after the final, Spain returned home.

Madrid was red and gold. The bus moved through streets filled with people. Eventually, the champions reached Cibeles, where Rodri took the microphone.

And then, amid all the celebrations, Spain’s captain, Rodri, stopped to speak about Ferran. He told the crowd:

“Hey everyone, today I’d like to remember someone incredibly important to this group and someone very special to me. He’s a player who has faced a lot of unfair criticism over the years but today he rewrote a piece of Spanish football history.”

Then came the chant. “Ferran… Ferran… Ferran…”

For years, the noise around Ferran Torres was something he had to learn to survive. Opinions about the transfer fee.

Opinions about missed chances. Opinions about whether he belonged at Barcelona. Whether he was good enough for Spain. Whether another forward should play instead.

At his lowest point, he allowed too much of that noise inside. So, he asked for help.

Slowly, he climbed. He took someone else’s hand to come out of the “bottomless pit”. Not in a straight line. Ferran’s career has never been in a straight line.

Until one night, in the 106th minute of a World Cup final, the ball came to him and all that remained was the simplest thing in football on paper. A finish. Ferran did just that.

Spain became world champions. The noise around Ferran returned.

Only this time, it was a country of 47 million people singing his name.

title: Progress or standing still - where are England under Borthwick?
description: As the 2027 Rugby World Cup continues to creep closer, where are England under Steve Borthwick, and do their Nations Championship performances point to progress?
fullText:
Pressure was on England for their summer Nations Championship fixtures after the Rugby Football Union backed head coach Steve Borthwick in May, following the country's worst Six Nations performance since the tournament's expansion in 2000.

Given England managed only one victory in the Six Nations, a tough defeat to world champions South Africa at Ellis Park was never going to carry too much weight, only if wins followed against Fiji and Argentina.

They did - just - as Borthwick's side completed their "brutal" summer schedule, which included more than 25,000 miles of travel.

As the 2027 Rugby World Cup continues to creep closer, where are England under Borthwick, and do their Nations Championship performances point to progress?

Having received the highest number of cards (eight yellows, two of which combined for a red) of any team in a Six Nations campaign since Italy in 2002, the RFU flagged England's ill-discipline as an area that had to improve this summer.

Former scrum-half Matt Dawson described England's discipline as "verging on comical" after their number of yellow cards for the year reached 14 in the chaotic 31-24 controversial win over Argentina.

"I don't think now is the time to be digging into that," Borthwick said of his side's discipline in the aftermath of the victory in Santiago del Estero.

An emphatic 73-8 win over Fiji in Liverpool ended a five-game losing run and was the only match this year in which England did not receive a yellow card.

Tommy Freeman and Guy Pepper were sent to the sin-bin late on against the Springboks.

Former England scrum-half Danny Care pointed out on BBC's Rugby Union Weekly that, across eight matches in 2026, Borthwick's side have spent more than two hours of rugby without a full complement of players on the pitch.

"It is the same stuff. It is not thuggish behaviour or violence," Care said.

"It is the little ones that you do not get away with under pressure, and pressure makes you do silly things.

"They have to work it out quite quickly. The senior players and coaches have a big job to limit them, as they won't win Test matches against the best teams."

Argentina coach accuses England of lack of respect

Rugby Union Weekly: Have we learnt anything from the Nations Championship?

England's discipline 'verging on comical' - Dawson

In July, England scored 125 points - a tally inflated by Fiji's no-show and playing a half with 14 players.

England kicked the ball more than any other team in the Six Nations.

Borthwick's side also recorded the second-highest number of entries into the opposition 22 during the Six Nations, but averaged only 2.7 points from each visit.

Only Italy, with an average of 1.6 points, had a worse return, with the RFU calling for more cutting edge in attack this summer.

Lee Blackett's attack, which did trouble South Africa in patches, was at its free-flowing best against Fiji, but arguably looked most impressive in the first half against Argentina.

"That first half was brilliant. England dominated, commanded and controlled everything," added former England wing Ugo Monye.

England scored three first-half tries in a hostile atmosphere as wingers Tommy Freeman and Immanuel Feyi-Waboso, who missed this year's Six Nations through injury, looked dangerous throughout the game.

Feyi-Waboso, 23, was the standout performer. From 12 carries, he beat 13 defenders, made 116 metres and produced four line breaks.

His importance was underlined by the fact he started all four November Tests, which formed part of England's 12-game winning streak.

Staying fit has been Feyi-Waboso's problem, and England must manage him and Freeman - who has looked more threatening on the wing than at 13 - correctly over the next 12 months.

"That [Argentina] was Manny's [Feyi-Waboso] best game for England," Monye added.

"I don't know why it has taken England so long. Their gameplan to get him [Feyi-Waboso] into the game is everything that Northampton do with Tommy Freeman to get him into the game.

"There was a period where he may have thought his role in this England team was to chase kicks and try to get the ball back."

"You're like the negativity committee, so it's lovely coming and talking to you again."

Borthwick, usually measured with the media, had a rare bit of bite to him in Argentina.

The hosts were denied a shot at levelling the match when Bautista Delguy's 83rd-minute try was controversially chalked off.

Prior to the game, Borthwick was asked about his continuity in selection: "That's four new caps in the past two weeks - how many caps did you want?"

England also faced criticism in the build-up to the 2023 World Cup, which included five defeats in six games and a first-ever loss to Fiji.

Care played at the 2023 World Cup and recalls Borthwick creating "a narrative within the group that it's you against the world".

"We did that at the 2023 World Cup, and it worked," Care added.

"Internally, what Steve is really good at is galvanising the group by saying, 'Look, even the media are having a go at us now, let's go and prove everyone wrong.'

"I didn't mind him clapping back a little bit. At least it showed a bit of fight because what people have said about Steve before is they can't break through to him and he's almost a little bit robotic."

Borthwick leaned heavily on a group of experienced players, well used to criticism, to reach the semi-finals of the 2023 World Cup.

Since then, that squad has been rejuvenated with exciting young talents - such as Henry Pollock, Freeman and Feyi-Waboso - while depth continues to be built.

Captain Maro Itoje will return from a rare summer break refreshed, and his side have now experienced the highs of a 12-game winning run and the lows of a five-game losing streak.

Unprecedented summer travel has likely played a role in why the thrilling performance at the end of the Six Nations against France in Paris has not been replicated.

But sitting two points off top spot in the northern hemisphere section of the Nations Championship, Borthwick's side are in a strong position to welcome Australia, Japan and New Zealand to Allianz Stadium in November.

Nine games are in England's diary before the World Cup warm-up games, with the window for change now likely closing.

title: Ipswich complete £20m deal for Ghana winger Fatawu
description: Ipswich Town complete the £20m signing of Abdul Fatawu, who played in all four of Ghana's games at the World Cup.
fullText:
Ipswich Town have continued their preparations for their return to the Premier League by completing the £20m signing of Ghana winger Abdul Fatawu from Leicester City.

The 22-year-old has signed a five-year contract and joins Gary O'Neil's side after scoring 16 goals in 100 games for Leicester since joining from Sporting in 2023.

Fatawu has arrived at Portman Road with Ipswich willing to allow winger Jaden Philogene to leave, with the 24-year-old attracting interest from Europe.

He has a list of admirers - believed to include Bologna, Roma and Benfica - and Ipswich will sanction a transfer if a suitable offer comes in.

Fatawu is the Tractor Boys' fifth summer signing after striker Emersonn joined from Toulouse earlier this month for a club record fee of about £26m.

Defender Cedric Kipre and forward Chuba Akpom also signed on a permanent basis following successful loans last season while goalkeeper Kayne van Oevelen arrived from Dutch side FC Volendam.

"The ambition the club has is very exciting and I'm looking forward to being a part of that as we prepare for the Premier League season," said Fatawu.

"After speaking to the manager, I know what the expectation is and I'm looking forward to meeting my team-mates and starting work.

"I have played against Ipswich a few times during my time in England and I know how passionate the supporters are for the club, so I'm excited to play for them and show them what I can do."

Fatawu was part of the Foxes squad relegated from the Championship last season, with Leicester now preparing for their second season in the third tier of English football.

The Tractor Boys finished second in the Championship last season and host Sunderland in their top-flight opener on 22 August.

title: Fernandez defends Argentina as Messi questions remain
description: Midfielder Enzo Fernandez claims Argentina represented their nation "in the best possible way" in the World Cup.
fullText:
Midfielder Enzo Fernandez has claimed Argentina represented their nation "in the best possible way" during the World Cup, despite ugly scenes during and after their final loss to Spain.

Fernandez was sent off late in normal time for two yellow cards and Spain won 1-0 after extra time on Sunday in New Jersey.

His first booking was for showing dissent towards Slovenian referee Slavko Vincic and Fernandez was later dismissed for catching Pau Cubarsi with a challenge that sent the defender cartwheeling in the air.

But, in a statement on social media, the Chelsea midfielder did not mention the red card and said: "As time goes by, you realise there is something much bigger than just a result.

"For years, this group has represented the team in the best possible way. It teaches that competing isn't just about winning; it's about giving your all for the jersey and never giving up.

"It has been a privilege to be part of a group that always stepped up, competed at the highest level, and defended these colours with pride, humility, and commitment."

Keeper Martinez may 'step aside' from Argentina

Fifa opens investigation into Argentina post-final trouble

Members of the Argentina team and backroom staff were involved in altercations with Spain players who had begun to celebrate their World Cup success.

Fifa's disciplinary committee has appointed a disciplinary and ethics prosecutor to assess what happened and any player or coach involved could face suspension, while the Argentine Football Association may also be fined.

Nahuel Molina, Leandro Paredes, Thiago Almada and assistant coach Roberto Ayala all appeared to be involved, with Paredes pushing Spain's Eric Garcia by the throat.

Spain's Gavi tried to intervene, but was forced to the ground by Almada, before Paredes pushed Gavi to the turf by putting his hand in the Spaniard's face.

Ayala then looked to be holding Garcia around the throat, before appearing to strike Spain midfielder Dani Olmo.

While the match ended with ugly scenes broadcast around the world, questions remain over what happened behind closed doors prior to kick-off.

There has been widespread speculation on social media about what was said in the Argentina dressing room before the game, including whether Lionel Messi informed his team-mates that the final would be his last match for the national side.

It comes after Argentine television company RLC Noticias posted footage appearing to show Messi urging his team-mates to stay calm on their walk to the tunnel - and "forget about everything else".

In the footage, which has not been verified by the BBC, Messi is seen saying: "Let's go, boys, [let's keep] calm people, calm. The main thing is for us to remain calm, boys, that we remain calm.

"Let's just concentrate on playing, nothing else - calm, OK? Forget everything else.

"Let's just play, let's just concentrate on playing. Let's go."

The actions of the Argentina players on the pitch were heavily criticised by former England internationals.

Ex-England captain Alan Shearer said: "Paredes threw two or three haymakers into someone's face. He went for them. There is no place or space for that.

"We know how much it means to them and we know how much it hurts to lose, but there is a way to lose. Too many times we have seen that from Argentina. The reaction after the final whistle is terrible."

Former England goalkeeper Joe Hart added: "The game's done, they can have absolutely no complaints about what happened. Disgusting behaviour."

Argentina manager Lionel Scaloni attempted to calm the situation down in his post-match interview.

He said: "We are gracious in victory, and we must be gracious in defeat. Today, we are showing that we know how to lose.

"We lost the match, and we accept it, but that doesn't mean we stop living or forget everything we did to get here."

Argentina, the World Cup winners in 1978, 1986 and 2022, won their first seven matches in North America, beating Algeria, Austria, Jordan, Cape Verde, Egypt, Switzerland and England before losing to Spain.

Against Egypt, Argentina were 2-0 down before scoring three late goals to win and in the semi-final with England, the Three Lions led 1-0 until the 85th minute before goals from Fernandez and a Lautaro Martinez header gave them a 2-1 win.

Several media outlets in Argentina have suggested the final could be Scaloni's last in charge and called it the end of an era, with talisman Lionel Messi, in tears at the end, unlikely to play at another World Cup.

"The pain is immense, and this wound will take time to heal. But I also hold onto all the good things," Messi wrote on social media.

However, the team were still greeted by thousands of Argentina fans with an open-top bus parade through the streets of Buenos Aires.

Scaloni and 16 of his 26 players returned to the country and were welcomed by a red carpet and military band at Ezeiza International Airport.

Aston Villa goalkeeper Emiliano Martinez, who made 11 saves during the final, was among the players in Argentina, but Messi was one of those who did not make the trip, instead returning to Miami where he plays club football for Inter Miami.

title: Headaches for Silicon Valley as China chips away at the US’s lead in the AI race
description:
Google’s AI struggles scream trouble as new Chinese models (again) throw US tech dominance into question
Hello, I’m Blake Montgomery, writing to you after a double-header feature of Christopher Nolan’s The Odyssey and the World Cup final. What a great Sunday. Today in tech, we’re discussing how China is chipping away at the US’s lead in the AI race and how Silicon Valley’s workers are taking action to protect their jobs from AI.

‘What’s the point?
fullText:
Google’s AI struggles scream trouble as new Chinese models (again) throw US tech dominance into question

Hello, I’m Blake Montgomery, writing to you after a double-header feature of Christopher Nolan’s The Odyssey and the World Cup final. What a great Sunday. Today in tech, we’re discussing how China is chipping away at the US’s lead in the AI race and how Silicon Valley’s workers are taking action to protect their jobs from AI.

Google is struggling with the latest version of its Gemini model

 A Chinese company, Moonshot, put out a free, surprisingly capable model last week

 China’s new models yet again thrown the US dominance in AI into question

Google is months behind in its launch schedule for the new version of its flagship AI model, Gemini 3.5. According to Bloomberg, Google is attempting to improve Gemini’s coding abilities to catch up to Claude, the Anthropic bot that is widely considered the best AI coding tool. OpenAI released a new model at the start of July; Google had been expected to do the same in May. News of the delay sent Google’s shares sliding as investors questioned whether it could maintain its top position in the AI race.

A refreshed and newly capable Gemini might also have ameliorated the damage to the US’s reputation as the leader in AI this week – but didn’t.

As Google’s internal struggles became public, the Chinese startup Moonshot debuted an unexpectedly strong model, sending shivers through Silicon Valley and Washington DC alike. Moonshot’s Kimi K3 model featured surprisingly capable coding, particularly in a niche called “front-end coding”. “This may be the single biggest release of the year,” Anastasios Angelopoulos, the CEO of AI testing company Arena, said.

Unlike ChatGPT, Claude and Gemini, Kimi K3 is free to use and open-weight, meaning anyone can download it and run it locally on their own computers, as its core components are released to the public.

It’s been the general strategy of Chinese AI companies: open-source and open-weight models, beating US AI companies on price as Chinese manufacturers do, and mirroring the broader trade war of the US and China. DeepSeek inspired a major US market panic last year. Alibaba previewed its latest model, Qwen3.8 Max, on Sunday, showing off a similar number of parameters as Kimi K3. The company plans to publish the model’s weights soon, per Bloomberg.

The strategy has been made possible by Beijing, which subsidizes computing resources and power consumption for its domestic AI startups, allowing them to release their AI models for free. The basic logic goes that if these startups can’t compete on capability with US models, they can compete on cost and undercut the US parent companies, which need to earn a profit eventually.

It’s all causing major headaches in Silicon Valley. There are no longtime incumbents in the AI business to whom customers will default. If those buyers, especially businesses who need lots of generated code, can turn to cheap or free alternatives, there might be little need to spend big on brand-name bots. The South China Morning Post, which is itself owned by the Alibaba Group, reported last week: “As US AI costs soar, global businesses pivot to China’s low-cost, open-weight models.”

In the US, nerves are rattling. Hawkish Trump administration officials are rekindling their efforts to block any US access to Chinese AI models via de facto bans, commerce department rules or an executive order, Axios reported Monday.

OpenAI’s policy head for advanced AI, former Donald Trump adviser Dean Ball, wrote that Beijing’s open-weight approach to AI would lead to “full AI communism” with government-run datacenters powering AI as a public good. He denounced that future. OpenAI has been sounding the alarm about Chinese models for years, which reads as part political disagreement and part business strategy, since Chinese open-weight models threaten OpenAI’s projected profits.

“I would guess that the Trump Administration will at some point realize that their best strategy here would be to create large amounts of regulatory risk around the use of open-weight Chinese models,” Ball wrote.

There is no alignment between the US’s leading AI companies and the government players in the same industry. Emil Michael, the US Pentagon’s chief technology officer, responded to Ball: “Every industry/ecosystem has its supreme village idiot. Dean Ball is that for AI.”

As Beijing presents a unified front, the White House’s response is growing more scattered by the day. Trump himself had talked until recently of complete deregulation and never restraining any AI investment, research or releases. By contrast, the White House is restricting access – even allies’ access – to Anthropic and OpenAI’s advanced cybersecurity models. Even the personnel are changing. Chris Fall, the director of the Trump administration’s Center for AI Standards and Innovation, is resigning just three months into his tenure, Axios reported Monday.

Not all of the AI crowd is against these models’ incursion on US dominance. Bill Gurley, a longtime venture capitalist best known for his early backing of Uber, wrote of China’s AI models in the Washington Post: “They aren’t a security threat – they’re what competition looks like.”

China’s advanced AI does pose a risk to the global business of AI, which is, as yet, still a race without a clear winner. Cheap Chinese AI models may trump their expensive US counterparts. The entire US economy is tied tight to the AI industry, raising the stakes of the Chinese models’ capabilities. Undercutting the US’s AI frontrunners’ business could unsettle the entire US stock market even more so than it already has.

That competition is set to play out in the future. In the present, though, China’s recent releases have thrown the politics of AI in the US into chaos. That reads as the bigger win.

New York becomes first state to impose one-year pause on new AI datacenters

Trump rails against New York’s statewide datacenter moratorium

Albanese’s AI blueprint sparks calls for datacentre moratorium until new regulations in place

Employees are taking some of the US’s first collective actions to protect their jobs from AI

Last week, Meta workers filed a lawsuit against their former employer alleging that it automated their firing via algorithmic decision-making. Meanwhile, thousands of Google’s workers petitioned their CEO for layoff protections as the company shifts its spending from employees to AI infrastructure.

“Make no mistake: this is a company that is enjoying massive, unprecedented success,” Parul Koul, a Google software engineer and the president of the Alphabet Workers Union, said outside the company’s California headquarters. “These layoffs and cuts are not difficult decisions, but simply profit being put over the people that make this company run.”

These Silicon Valley employees are making these moves against the backdrop of the AI boom, which is changing software engineering rapidly and causing unions to gain popularity.

A Meta employee who was part of a recent union drive in the UK said: “It’s about us having a voice in all of the operations of the company, from products to employee experience.”

Meta countered last week’s lawsuit with an unusually direct statement: “These claims lack merit and are not based on facts. Workforce management and organizational decisions were and are made by people, not AI.” Google did not issue a statement in response to last week’s petition.

We’ve seen Silicon Valley luminaries warn of futuristic apocalyptic scenarios, but we’re seeing those worries come to life among the fearful rank-and-file employees, who are now taking action. We’re witnessing how those workforces, who have a front-row seat to AI’s development, respond to its effects on their employment.

Read more from Reworked, a Guardian series about what’s at stake as AI disrupts our jobs.

title: Keeper Martinez may 'step aside' from Argentina
description: Argentina goalkeeper Emi Martinez says he is considering whether it is "time to step aside" from international football after his side's 1-0 defeat to Spain in the World Cup final.
fullText:
Argentina goalkeeper Emi Martinez says he is considering whether it is "time to step aside" from international football after his side's 1-0 defeat to Spain in the World Cup final.

The 33-year-old has won 67 caps for his country and played every minute of Argentina's eight World Cup games, keeping two clean sheets and conceding eight goals.

He was a World Cup winner in 2022, also claiming the golden glove award as the tournament's outstanding goalkeeper.

"I dreamt we'd win it again, I dreamt of bringing it back to Argentina and making history once more," Martinez wrote in a post on Instagram., external

"The truth is, the pain is hard to explain. There are many things to reflect on and how to move forward, and if it's time to step aside.

"I'm so sorry, I truly tried my best to help my country and my team-mates."

Martinez won the Yashin Trophy, awarded to the world's best goalkeeper at the Ballon d'Or awards, in 2023 and 2024.

He has played for Aston Villa since 2020 and has a contract until 2029, but has been linked to a move to Italian side Juventus this summer.

title: Why World Cup can be game-changer for ambitious MLS
description: Can the success of the 2026 World Cup help MLS become one of the world's elite leagues?
fullText:
"Thanks world, We'll take it from here."

That's the message from Lionel Messi, Son Heung-min, David Beckham and dozens of Major League Soccer players in an advertising campaign urging new football fans created by the World Cup to check out MLS.

The slick film showcases World Cup stars who play in the US top flight, inviting fans to join them as the regular season resumes. The league is even offering free tickets to new fans.

"The World Cup has now exposed millions of people to Major League Soccer," says MLS executive vice-president Dan Courtemanche, who joined the league in 1995 before a ball was kicked.

"We're going to continue to capitalise on that momentum."

Could the lasting legacy of this World Cup be the rise of MLS into the band of elite leagues?

Major League Soccer is itself a legacy product from the 1994 World Cup, when Fifa mandated the US to establish a professional league as part of its bid to host the tournament.

The league started in 1996 with 10 teams and zero purpose-built stadiums. Thirty years later, MLS has 30 clubs and almost as many football-specific stadiums.

Despite predictions of empty World Cup seats because of sky-high ticket prices, the stadiums were packed, and broadcast ratings were impressive as football joy spread across the United States - even after the USA were knocked out of the tournament.

The US team's loss to Belgium at the last-16 stage delivered the most-watched football game on one network in US history - with more than 33 million watching on Fox.

England's last-16 win over Mexico became the most-watched football game in US history with a combined 45 million viewers in English and Spanish watching across Fox and Telemundo.

The ratings compare with games in the NFL - what Americans consider football - but are not up to the level of the showpiece Super Bowl.

In 2025, the most-watched Super Bowl in history attracted 127.7 million viewers across English and Spanish broadcasts and streaming in the US, although many Americans tune in solely for the half-time show, which was performed by Kendrick Lamar that year.

MLS, which has shown strong growth since its first season 30 years ago, aspires to become an elite league on par with Europe's top competitions.

"I think we're looked at as the top, one of the top leagues commercially," MLS commissioner Don Garber said at an MLS event to discuss the future of the league and the post-World Cup legacy. "We've cracked that code. Now we've got to crack the rest of it."

In some ways, MLS is already up there with the big leagues across the world.

There were 45 MLS players represented at the 2026 World Cup - including two in the final for Argentina, Messi and Rodrigo de Paul.

MLS has a reputation for being almost a retirement home for superstars - a lucrative place to play for legends like Beckham and Messi after peak careers in Europe.

But that reputation is outdated, Courtemanche says, with the average age of new international players in the league now 23.8 years. Though many of them have dreams of playing in Europe after experiencing MLS.

MLS is changing its calendar in 2027 to adopt a summer-to-spring season, more aligned with Europe to make it easier for players to move between the leagues.

And the competition's leaders are investing in youth development leagues.

All MLS teams run youth academies. As LA Galaxy hosted a viewing party for the US victory over Bosnia-Herzegovina on a big screen, dozens of children ignored the match, instead battling it out themselves on three separate pitches, occasionally running to the big screen when the sound of a goal spread through the crowd.

These were rare pick-up games in a culture full of highly scheduled children - and the skill level was high. Some of the kids played for MLS youth teams.

Eight-year-old Landon - named after US star Landon Donovan - was one of the more skilled, moving from goalkeeper to forward with glee. His mother Jen Wing is a coach and says her children have attended MLS camps and play on travel teams at their home in New Jersey.

Landon dreams of being a professional soccer player one day in MLS.

"LAFC," he says, referring to his dream team.

But critics say MLS will never compete with Europe until it sorts out its "pay-to-play" culture in youth development.

At the top level, MLS academies train and develop youth players for free, much like their European counterparts.

But to get to that level, where an MLS academy team would notice a teenage player, families typically pay thousands of dollars for private lessons and access to travel teams, a common theme in US sports.

MLS officials say the development leagues are constantly evolving and improving when it comes to developing young talent, and that every team has scholarships available to talented players.

Commissioner Garber says the league is always improving and is determined to get even better at developing young players so they can deliver "more and more to the men's national team and just be better, bigger, stronger, faster, all those things that give you energy to think of a better future".

When it comes to attracting audiences, Messi is the league's biggest draw.

But the cult of celebrity runs throughout the league. More than two dozen entertainers and athletes hold ownership stakes in MLS clubs, including Will Ferrell (LAFC), Reese Witherspoon (Nashville SC), Matthew McConaughey (Austin FC) and Magic Johnson (LAFC).

Drew Carey was an original Seattle Sounders owner when the club joined MLS in 2009 for a $30m (£22m) expansion fee. The Sounders are now valued at more than $900m (£668m). David Beckham's 2007 LA Galaxy contract included the option to buy an MLS expansion team for $25m. He now owns Inter Miami CF, a club that experts have valued at more than a billion dollars.

But as much as the US is experiencing a football frenzy right now, many believe US culture will always prioritise American football.

Brian Udovich, whose 12-year-old son Croil plays in an MLS Next Level league in Southern California, says many talented youngsters leave the sport for American football when they become teenagers because of all the fanfare.

On Friday nights, American football players get their names up in lights with marching bands and cheerleaders urging them on to glory in giant stadiums. Whereas soccer is typically played on Saturday mornings with mums and dads in folding chairs.

"It just means so much more to other cultures than us," Udovich says.

Michael Bradley, coach of the New York Red Bulls, says American culture is just different and that the legacy of the World Cup will make association football more prominent.

"We don't have the long, storied football history that other countries do. I think for a long time that led to an inferiority complex that maybe football would never really survive in the United States," Bradley told the BBC, adding that now so many more fans in the US feel proud.

"I think we can all unequivocally say in 2026, football is not going anywhere."

title: Report: Man United are leading the race to sign World Cup midfielder
description: Manchester United Hold Early Edge in Manu Kone Transfer RaceManchester United appear to be in a promising position as the market for Manu Kone begins to develop, with L’Equipe reporting that the Fra...
fullText:
Manchester United appear to be in a promising position as the market for Manu Kone begins to develop, with L’Equipe reporting that the France international is giving serious thought to the next step in his career.

Kone, now 25, comes into this summer at an important moment. After two solid seasons with Roma, where he has made 82 appearances, and after helping the Italian side secure third place with 73 points last term, the midfielder has strengthened his standing. His World Cup ended painfully with France’s 2-0 semi-final defeat to Spain, and the emotional aftermath underlined how deeply he had invested in the campaign. Yet that tournament also seems to have reinforced the sense that he is ready for a bigger platform.

The central point is clear. Kone wants to test himself at a higher level, ideally with a club carrying strong European ambitions. Inter Milan have tracked the situation for some time, according to reporting in Italy, but the strongest likelihood now appears to be a move to the Premier League.

Among the English clubs with interest, Manchester United are described as having something of a head start. Initial talks have taken place, although the situation has not yet moved into advanced territory. That reflects a wider theme in this summer window, with clubs across Europe operating carefully after a market slowed by the World Cup.

United’s recruitment team are understood to be assessing a number of midfield options, and Kone is firmly among the priority names on that shortlist. The club’s stance is measured. There is a determination to avoid overpaying and to weigh up each move properly before accelerating. In that context, interest should be taken seriously, but not yet viewed as definitive.

From Kone’s side, there is believed to be genuine openness to Manchester United. The attraction of English football has been there for some time, and his current market is strongest in the Premier League. Arsenal have also been mentioned previously, while two other English sides are said to have expressed interest, even if their identities remain undisclosed.

The player would prefer clarity sooner rather than later, particularly after a demanding international summer. Whether that arrives quickly is another matter. For now, Manchester United seem well placed, but this remains a transfer race still in its early stages.

As a Manchester United supporter, this is exactly the sort of report that grabs the attention. Manu Kone feels like a player who fits what the squad has lacked in midfield for too long, energy, athleticism, intensity and the ability to cover ground without sacrificing quality on the ball. If the club are serious about refreshing the engine room, he looks like a very sensible target.

What stands out most is his apparent openness to the move. That matters. United have spent too many windows chasing players who needed convincing, and that rarely ends well. If Kone sees Old Trafford as the right next step, then the club should recognise that opportunity and act decisively.

At the same time, there is some reassurance in the idea that United do not want to rush or overpay. Supporters have seen too many expensive deals made from panic rather than planning. A calmer process is welcome, as long as it does not become hesitation for its own sake.

From a football perspective, Kone could bring balance and drive. He looks ready for the Premier League, and his age suggests there is still another level to come. If United want midfielders who can compete physically, progress the ball and grow into major roles, this feels like one worth pushing hard for.

title: Lionel Messi’s Wife Sends Heartfelt Message After World Cup Final Loss
description: After what was likely Lionel Messi’s final World Cup appearance for Argentina, Antonela Roccuzzo shared a touching post about her husband. The post Lionel Messi’s Wife Sends Heartfelt Message After World Cup Final Loss appeared first on HEAVY.
fullText:
Although Argentina fell short of a second consecutive World Cup victory on Sunday, Lionel Messi had no reason to hang his head—a sentiment shared by his teammates, fans and most of all, his wife.

After what was likely Messi’s final World Cup appearance for La Albiceleste, Antonela Roccuzzo shared a two-photo post featuring a teary-eyed image of her husband alongside one of his teammates tossing him into the air. It was her way of suggesting that the good comes with the bad, while celebrating just how proud she was of him.

The photos were accompanied by a lengthy message directed to Messi.

“You will always be the best, @leomessi,” she beegan.

“Not just because of your talent, but because you have never stopped being yourself. Because no matter what happens, you never give up; you always fight to the very end and give it your all until the final second,” the caption read in Spanish (h/t NYPost).

“That strength, that mindset, and the way you pick yourself up time and time again are what make you unique. 🤍 Thank you for showing us every day that true success is built through hard work, sacrifice, and perseverance—without ever losing sight of who you really are.”

Antonela went on to praise the always-humble Messi for being a model citizen and a role model to their three children and millions of young fans across the world.

“You are the best example for our children and an inspiration to millions of people,” she continued “I admire you more than words can express, and I feel immensely proud to walk through life by your side. I love you so much. 🤍”

While Messi has yet to officially announce his retirement from international football, his social media post following the loss hinted at just that.

“The pain is immense, and it will take time for this wound to heal,” he wrote on IG.

Much like his wife, Messi cherished the good times leading into the World Cup final, which saw Argentina lose 1-0 to Spain in extra time.

“But I also hold on to all the good things. Today it is difficult to appreciate what we accomplished, but this group reached two consecutive World Cup finals,” he wrote, beaming with pride about his country’s accomplishments.

Messi went on to thank his teammates and fans for the wonderful journey

“Thank you from the bottom of my heart for every greeting and every message,” he added. “Once again, we managed to unite as a country and stand together, sharing the immense pride of being Argentine.”

title: Barcelona midfielder Gavi shares heartfelt message after World Cup glory: ‘I owe a great deal to everyone at La Masia’
description: Spain’s historic FIFA World Cup triumph continues to produce unforgettable moments, and Barcelona midfielder Gavi has now shared one of the most emotional stories to emerge from the celebrations. J...
fullText:
Spain’s historic FIFA World Cup triumph continues to produce unforgettable moments, and Barcelona midfielder Gavi has now shared one of the most emotional stories to emerge from the celebrations.

Just hours after helping Spain defeat Argentina 1-0 in the 2026 FIFA World Cup final, the 21-year-old reflected on his remarkable journey from a young footballer in Los Palacios to a world champion.

The midfielder shared a heartfelt social media post, where he thanked the people who influenced his career while looking back on the sacrifices that helped him reach football’s biggest stage.

Following Spain’s victory over Argentina, Gavi shared two photographs that perfectly captured his football journey.

One showed him playing as a youngster for La Liara Balompie in his hometown of Los Palacios, while the other featured him kissing the FIFA World Cup trophy after Spain were crowned world champions.

Accompanying the images was an emotional message that quickly resonated with supporters across the football world.

“May no one wake me from this dream – the dream of a boy from Los Palacios who dreamed of winning the World Cup with his country…”

The Barcelona midfielder also reflected on the setbacks he overcame before reaching the pinnacle of international football.

After battling injuries during his young career, Gavi admitted that every difficult moment had been worthwhile as he celebrated becoming a Spain World Cup winner at just 21 years of age.

Gavi made sure to acknowledge everyone who helped him along the way, explaining the role his family, close friends and former clubs played in his development.

Gavi has thanked everyone who influenced his career. (Photo by David Ramos/Getty Images)

The Spanish international thanked Real Betis, where his football journey began, before reserving special praise for FC Barcelona and La Masia, the academy that helped transform him into one of Europe’s brightest midfielders.

“I owe a great deal to everyone at La Masia who welcomed me from day one, and to the coaches and staff at FC Barcelona, who helped me grow as a footballer and as a person, and enabled me to become a world champion,” he wrote.

The Barcelona academy graduate also dedicated part of his message to his grandmother, thanking her for “that extra strength you gave me over the last few months.”

Although Gavi was not a regular starter during the tournament, he still contributed whenever called upon by Spain manager Luis de la Fuente.

The midfielder played 76 minutes across the competition, including a start against Cape Verde and a late appearance in the knockout victory over Austria.
