title: Could Infantino really push through a 64-team World Cup?
description: If we have learned one thing from Gianni Infantino's Fifa tenure, it is that more is better. That could mean 64 teams at the World Cup.
fullText:
If we have learned one thing from 10 years of Gianni Infantino as Fifa president, it is that more is better.

More tournaments, more games, more money.

The new-look Club World Cup, the expanded World Cup, the inflated ticket prices.

And, of course, the Super Bowl-style show that meant the half-time break at the World Cup final lasted 27 minutes and 22 seconds.

Infantino appears to have thrown the door wide open to a 64-team World Cup.

"If you don't give smaller countries a chance to participate, they'll lack the incentive to keep improving," he said.

Except, of course, smaller countries are not denied the chance. If they are good enough, they qualify. See Cape Verde, Curacao, Jordan and Uzbekistan.

Purely in terms of the competition format, it does make sense.

The top two teams in the 16 groups would go through to the knockout stage. No longer would there be a need to send a few third-placed teams through.

But is there really any other logical reason to expand to 64 teams?

Let's start with how we got here.

The 2030 World Cup will mark 100 years since the first World Cup, which was held in Uruguay.

The tournament will primarily be hosted by Morocco, Portugal and Spain - but the opening three matches will be played in Uruguay, Argentina and Paraguay as centenary hosts.

Conmebol - the South American confederation - wants more games.

In April 2025, it requested a one-off tournament of 64 teams so it could take on the full programme of the additional four groups.

Its president, Alejandro Dominguez, said it would ensure "nobody on the planet is left out of the party".

Within hours of the 2026 tournament ending, Dominguez was again reiterating his desire for an expanded World Cup. In a series of posts on social media he said Conmebol was "working to ensure that the 2030 World Cup features 64 teams".

"International football grows when it opens up to the world," he added.

There has been little support from elsewhere.

Uefa president Aleksander Ceferin dismissed it as a "bad idea" for both the tournament and qualifying process.

Victor Montagliani - president of the governing body of North and Central America and the Caribbean (Concacaf) - said last year it would damage "the broader football ecosystem".

Asian Football Confederation (AFC) president Sheikh Salman bin Ibrahim Al Khalifa agreed, saying further expansion could bring "chaos".

None of which means it will not happen, with the decision taken by the 37 members of the Fifa Council.

A 64-team tournament has two main plus points: a more natural format, and more opportunities for emerging nations.

It is the latter which Infantino is championing, especially after the heroics of Cape Verde.

Fifa faced criticism before the tournament, with suggestions the additional teams would not be competitive.

Then Cape Verde drew with Spain and Uruguay to finish second in their group. And the Blue Sharks gave Argentina an almighty scare by taking them to extra time in the last 16, before losing 3-2.

But the fairytale stories must be balanced with the competitive edge of the tournament.

Haiti, Iraq, Jordan, Panama, Tunisia and Uzbekistan failed to pick up a point - and this summer's group stage had minimal jeopardy for the top nations.

Add more teams and it would be difficult to see a repeat of top-four seeds like Belgium (2022), Germany (2018), Spain (2014), Italy (2010) and France (2002) failing to make to knockout stage.

Then there is the question of where the extra places would go.

Based on this year's qualifying, these are the additional countries who would have made it into a 64-team World Cup 2026 (with their Fifa ranking at the start of the tournament in brackets):

AFC: Indonesia (118), Oman (79), United Arab Emirates (68).

Uefa: Denmark (21), Italy (12), Poland (36).

Concacaf: Honduras (65), Jamaica (71), Suriname (125).

Conmebol: Bolivia (77), Venezuela (49).

Africa: Burkina Faso (62), Cameroon (44), Gabon (86), Nigeria (26).

Six of those teams are from the top 50 of Fifa's world rankings - three from Europe, two from Africa and one from South America.

And therein lies the problem. Asia and Concacaf are weaker confederations, as was proven by performances this year.

Nine of Africa's 10 countries got out of the group stage. But only two of nine Asian countries advanced, and just three of the six Concacaf countries - the co-hosts - made it through.

New Zealand, who got one point and finished bottom of their group, are the only country from Oceania in the world's top 150.

There is an argument that if you extend again, you should give a greater weight of places to Europe just for competitive balance.

But, on the other hand, there are those who would argue that bringing more teams into the World Cup ultimately allows the game to develop further in those countries.

Infantino will sell the expansion as being good for the global game, but there have to be doubts that it is good for the competition.

Almost a third of Fifa's 211 nations would be present.

Would the group stage not simply become a glorified pan-confederation qualifying competition for what is, essentially, a knockout tournament?

Expanding the World Cup to 64 teams is really just a case of bolting on four additional four-team groups.

But more groups means more time.

This year's tournament lasted 39 days. An additional 24 matches would require 20 schedule slots. Or another five days.

But this summer's event was unique for two reasons: multiple time zones and indoor stadiums.

Fifa was able to elongate a matchday at either end, with games scheduled over a 14-hour period so it could stay true to exclusive time windows for television coverage.

That will not be so straightforward in the future, even with 48 teams.

Climate change is making playing in the daytime more challenging - see the record heatwaves in Europe in recent weeks.

Other countries are far less likely to have the covered venues of Atlanta, Dallas Houston and Vancouver to mitigate it.

The Saudi Arabia World Cup is likely to move to January 2035 but it is not clear how other countries will get around this growing problem.

Until this year, only once had there been dual hosts of the World Cup - Japan and South Korea in 2002.

It is hard to imagine any one country ever being capable of hosting again. Even Saudi Arabia.

Could we be looking at World Cups hosted by, for instance, the UK and Scandinavia? Or Eastern Europe?

How would an Asian or African country be able to fund hosting the World Cup?

Let's look a just a few issues that would need to be resolved.

This summer there were 16 host cities with stadiums having capacities ranging from 43,000 to 81,000. More would be required.

There would need to be 64 individual, elite-level training facilities for the qualifying nations.

Then there is the provision of hotels for the fans, and the infrastructure and support network to move them around.

Expanding the World Cup is not as simple as giving more opportunities to more countries.

One of Infantino's key promises when he was elected was to bring more money into the game.

Every football association now has access to $8m (£5.9m) in funding over a four-year period, with a further $1.2m (£890,000) on top for the smaller nations.

Confederations get $60m (£44.5m) to develop, promote and organise football.

It is why Infantino is likely to be re-elected unopposed next year.

This money has to come from somewhere, primarily the World Cup.

In 2017, Fifa projected a 48-team World Cup would bring in $6.5bn (£4.8bn).

In 2022, it revised the format again and added 24 games. Now, it expects to rake in $9bn (£6.7bn) this year.

A 64-team event is likely to raise more from television, sponsorship and ticket sales.

Fifa says it has a responsibility to grow the game, and its tool for this appears to be more and more football.

But critics would argue there has to be a point where expansion is no longer good for the game.

title: Ferran Torres seemingly mocks Donald Trump with ‘Make Spain Great Again’ at FIFA World Cup parade
description: Ferran Torres bagged the winner for Spain in the FIFA World Cup final, and used the parade to make a statement against Donald Trump. Spain were presented the World Cup trophy by Trump, who was then at the centre of an awkward moment as he refused to leave the Spanish National Team’s trophy-raising moment.
fullText:
Ferran Torres bagged the winner for Spain in the FIFA World Cup final, and used the parade to make a statement against Donald Trump.

Spain were presented the World Cup trophy by Trump, who was then at the centre of an awkward moment as he refused to leave the Spanish National Team’s trophy-raising moment.

It appears this, along with some other moments of controversy throughout the tournament, has not sat well with Ferran Torres, as the Barcelona star took his chance at the parade to mock the current US President.

After the FIFA World Cup concluded, the Spanish National Team were given the customary heroes’ welcome when they returned to Madrid.

As is tradition, the Spanish National Team held a parade to celebrate their monumental victory. During the parade, Torres could be seen sporting a red baseball cap that read ‘Make Spain Great Again.’

The cap was an obvious parody of the red ‘Make America Great Again’ caps that are associated with Donald Trump, who coined the slogan as part of both his 2016 and 2024 Presidential campaigns.

Given the highly scrutinised nature of Trump’s involvement in the FIFA World Cup, including the trophy ceremony, it is very likely that Torres is taking a dig at the 45th and 47th President of the United States.

Donald Trump has been visibly involved with the FIFA World Cup throughout the tournament, but his involvement goes way back.

Trump spearheaded the United States’ bid for the tournament back in 2017 and was instrumental in making sure that the bid was successful.

Since then, FIFA has presented him with the inaugural FIFA Peace Prize, a move that garnered a lot of criticism from fans and officials around the world.

Of course, the most notable incident came earlier this summer, when Trump reportedly influenced FIFA to overturn Folarin Balogun’s ban in time for their Round of 16 tie against Belgium.

Many saw it as Trump leveraging his political and financial power over FIFA president Gianni Infantino to give the USMNT an edge.

While the USMNT didn’t win with Balogun on the field, many felt that Trump and the USMNT had violated the spirit and ethical code of the game and the tournament.

title: 'Kick Argentina out of the World Cup' petition nearly breaks Guinness World Record
description: FIFA has confirmed that Argentina will face an investigation into the “disgusting” scenes at the end of the World Cup final. The team has gone viral for all the wrong reasons at this year’s tournament; and now, a petition to kick them out of the World Cup has amassed more than 23 million signatures.View on euronews
fullText:
What a fun World Cup this has been... If only FIFA and Argentina hadn't ruined the whole thing.

It's a sentiment shared by many online, who believe that this year's tournament has brought the culture of football further into disrepute - whether it's the exorbitant ticket prices, the controversial "hydration breaks", the Trump-Infantino relationship leading to the infamous red card scandal, some dubious refereeing and allegations of favouritism... The list goes on, and went on up until the final, in which Spain defeated Argentina 1-0 in extra time.

Indeed, FIFA has confirmed that Argentina will face an investigation regarding altercations with Spain players, with players like Nahuel Molina, Leandro Paredes and Thiago Almada appearing to be involved, as well as assistant coach Roberto Ayala.

Molina appeared to throw a punch at Spain’s captain Rodri as he ran past him, while Paredes clashed with Spain’s Eric Garcia.

Faced with these pathetic scenes, FIFA's disciplinary committee has appointed an ethics prosecutor to assess what happened.

The actions of the Argentinian team could be judged as "violating the basic rules of decent conduct" and "behaving in a way that brings the sport of football and/or Fifa into disrepute".

Vikings, Trump cards and baby fans: The 10 biggest viral moments of the World Cup 2026

Donald Trump photobombing La Roja goes viral following Spain's World Cup victory

This has been a long time coming, as supporting Argentina during the World Cup became increasingly problematic as the competition progressed. The team and their fans went viral for all the wrong reasons – be it racist chants, some questionable calls during the quarter-final game against Egypt which seemed to suggest favouritism, a banner that read "Las Malvinas son Argentinas" ("The Falklands are Argentine") during their post-semifinal celebration against England - to name but a few.

The conspiracy theory that the World Cup was rigged in Argentina's favour gained much traction online, with many expressing their "disgust" and anger through AI-generated videos and photos.

Take a look at some examples below:

The aforementioned disciplinary and ethics prosecutor has the power to enforce a ban globally, and a petition titled "Argentina Out" has called for the country to be kicked out of the tournament permanently.

The petition description accuses FIFA and the referees of being "biased toward Lionel Messi and Argentina", adding: "Why should the rest of the world compete when the winner has already been decided? Kick Argentina out of the World Cup and give everyone else a fair chance."

Now closed, the appeal has more than 23 million signatures - 23,316,108 to be precise.

Most impressive of all is how this has nearly broken a world record, as the Guinness World Record for the most signatories stands at 24,319,181.

That record still belongs to the 1997 Jubilee 2000 movement, which called for the cancellation of unpayable debts owed by the world's poorest countries by the end of the year 2000.

"Over 23 million people from 170 countries signed this petition in under a week," states the viral Argentina Out petition. "FIFA ignored us but Spain delivered. Thank you Spain."

title: South American football boss calls for 64 teams in 2030
description: South American football president Alejandro Domínguez says it would be a great opportunity to mark the World Cup’s 100th anniversary in 2030 with 64 teams. The next men's tournament will held in Spain, Portugal, Morocco, Argentina, Uruguay and Paraguay.
fullText:
South American football president Alejandro Domínguez says it would be a great opportunity to mark the World Cup’s 100th anniversary in 2030 with 64 teams.

The next men's tournament will held in Spain, Portugal, Morocco, Argentina, Uruguay and Paraguay. The latter three will have opening games to commemorate the first ever tournament in Uruguay in 1930, before action switches over the Atlantic.

But the number of teams has yet to be confirmed and FIFA president Gianni Infantino hinted during the recent 2026 edition, held with a record 48 teams, that he could envisage 64 in four years' time.

Domínguez is adamant it can work.

"The next match will be played at home!", the head of the South American continental confederation CONMEBOL wrote on X late on Monday.

"In 2030, the World Cup will be held in Uruguay, Argentina and Paraguay – a great opportunity for football to celebrate the World Cup’s 100th anniversary with a tournament featuring 64 teams."

The 2030 event is currently planned for June 8 to July 21 and would be the longest World Cup in history at 44 days.

The 48-team tournament meant an extra round of 32 and had the eight best third-placed nations from the groups going through. A 64-team event would make more sense mathematically with only group winners and second-placed teams going through to the knock-outs and no extra games per team compared to this year.

title: Report: Aston Villa considering move to sign World Cup forward
description: Aston Villa Eye Crysencio Summerville as Morgan Rogers Exit NearsAston Villa are weighing up a move for Crysencio Summerville, with the West Ham winger emerging as a serious option if Morgan Rogers co...
fullText:
Aston Villa are weighing up a move for Crysencio Summerville, with the West Ham winger emerging as a serious option if Morgan Rogers completes his blockbuster switch to Chelsea. According to talkSPORT, Villa are now considering whether to enter a growing race for the £40m-rated Netherlands international.

The timing is significant. Rogers is reportedly closing in on a £117m transfer to Stamford Bridge, a deal set to become a record fee for a British player. With that expected departure likely to alter Villa’s attacking structure, attention has quickly turned towards possible replacements and Summerville appears firmly in the frame.

There is already established competition. Roma have tabled an offer worth around £40m for the 24-year-old, while Manchester United and Tottenham have also been linked. Villa’s interest, therefore, places them in a crowded market for a player whose situation has changed sharply following West Ham’s relegation to the Championship.

Summerville is set to leave the London Stadium after the Hammers dropped into England’s second tier, and that has opened the door to a transfer through a release clause in his contract. For clubs looking for a dynamic winger entering his peak years, that naturally makes him an attractive proposition.

The former Leeds forward managed seven goals in 34 appearances in all competitions during a difficult campaign for West Ham. Even so, he was described as “a bright spark in an otherwise disappointing season”, and there is a feeling across the market that his best football remains ahead of him.

His profile has only strengthened after the World Cup in North America. Summerville contributed two goals and two assists as Holland reached the round of 32 before losing to Morocco on penalties. Those displays appear to have reinforced the belief among interested clubs that he can operate at a higher level and within a more stable attacking unit.

From Villa’s perspective, the appeal is obvious. Summerville offers directness, carries genuine one-v-one threat and can stretch opponents from wide areas. If Rogers leaves, replacing output is important, but so is replacing unpredictability, and Summerville brings exactly that.

This will not be straightforward. Manchester United are understood to have opened talks earlier this month, although any move is said to depend on the future of Marcus Rashford. Tottenham also hold a long-standing interest and had already made an enquiry in January.

Roma, meanwhile, are pushing hard. The Italian club have already shown a willingness to do business around Villa, having taken Donyell Malen on an initial loan in January before completing a permanent £21.3m deal in May. Malen’s 15 goals in 20 games in all competitions only strengthens Roma’s confidence in targeting Premier League-based talent.

That leaves Villa needing to decide whether to move quickly or risk missing out. If Rogers does depart for the reported £117m, there would be money available, though spending power must still be viewed through the lens of squad planning and profit and sustainability rules.

That point was raised directly when the potential fit was discussed earlier this month. Speaking to talkSPORT, Andy Brassell said: “You just wonder if the right players are around him, the right stage on which to compete, he could really get there.” He added: “I wonder if he could be a potential Aston Villa player going into next season.”

Those comments go to the heart of this story. Villa are not simply searching for a like-for-like replacement, they are looking for someone who can be elevated by the environment around him. Brassell also said: “Now of course we know that their hands are tied a little bit with PSR and they can’t go out splashing out infinite amounts to improve their squad.”

That feels especially relevant. Even if the Rogers deal goes through, Villa will want to be precise rather than extravagant. Summerville may sit in the right bracket, both financially and tactically. As Brassell put it: “I tend to see him as more of an inside player than an outside player, which Summerville is even though he can come into those central areas as well. So I wonder if that could be a good get for them.”

There is also a wider endorsement of the player’s trajectory. Brassell concluded: “But there are a number of teams in the Premier League who would really benefit from Summerville, and I think with the right situation around him he’s ready to go and become the player that a lot of people believed he could be when he came into the Premier League with Leeds in first place.”

From an Aston Villa supporter’s perspective, this is the sort of link that immediately grabs the attention. If Morgan Rogers is genuinely on his way for £117m, replacing that quality and energy is no small task, but Summerville feels like a clever name to consider. He is proven in English football, still only 24, and there is a sense that his ceiling has not yet been reached.

The most encouraging part of the report is the fit. Villa need wide players who can attack space, commit defenders and give the side more thrust in transition. Summerville does all of that. He has had to perform in difficult circumstances before, and that can harden a player. Put him into a stronger side with better structure and more support, and there is every chance his numbers rise.

There will be understandable concerns about the £40m valuation, especially with PSR in the background, but if Rogers goes for the kind of fee being discussed, Villa have to be ambitious. This is not about replacing one player pound for pound, it is about ensuring the squad remains dangerous and progressive.

Supporters would probably accept that there are bigger names on the market, but this kind of move often makes more sense. Summerville has hunger, Premier League experience and room to improve. If Villa can move decisively, this has the look of a smart post-Rogers rebuild move.

title: Barcelona captain set for crucial medical assessment after World Cup injury – report
description: Barcelona are closing in on a crucial decision regarding Frenkie de Jong’s injury, with the Dutch midfielder set to return to the Ciutat Esportiva Joan Gamper in the coming days for further medical ...
fullText:
Barcelona are closing in on a crucial decision regarding Frenkie de Jong’s injury, with the Dutch midfielder set to return to the Ciutat Esportiva Joan Gamper in the coming days for further medical assessment.

According to SPORT, the Barcelona midfielder will undergo another round of evaluations before the club’s medical staff and the player jointly decide on the most suitable treatment plan.

The latest Barcelona news comes after De Jong returned from the 2026 FIFA World Cup carrying a knee problem that worsened during the Netherlands’ campaign.

Although the initial outlook remains concerning, Barcelona have already received one significant piece of encouraging news as they continue to monitor one of Hansi Flick’s most important players.

Frenkie de Jong’s injury has been one of the biggest concerns for Barcelona since the midfielder returned from international duty.

De Jong played the final two matches of the Netherlands’ World Cup campaign despite experiencing considerable discomfort in his right knee.

Frenkie de Jong played the World Cup with a knee injury. (Photo by David Ramos/Getty Images)

Following the tournament, he reported to the Ciutat Esportiva on the opening day of Barcelona’s pre-season, where club doctors immediately examined the affected knee.

Medical staff initially identified an injury, but the level of swelling prevented them from completing every necessary test.

Once the inflammation reduced, further examinations confirmed that the problem was more serious than first hoped.

However, the biggest positive for Barcelona was that doctors ruled out any major damage to the knee ligaments, avoiding what could have been a far longer spell on the sidelines.

The Dutch international was subsequently advised to continue his holiday while following conservative treatment and allowing the knee to rest before returning for a final assessment later this week.

De Jong could be out for three to four months. (Photo by Molly Darlington/Getty Images)

The latest Barcelona injury news suggests De Jong is expected to miss between three and four months, meaning he is likely to sit out the opening phase of the new campaign.

Once he returns to the training ground, Barcelona’s medical department and the midfielder will agree on the next stage of his rehabilitation programme before confirming the definitive treatment strategy.

Despite losing one of the team’s most influential players, Hansi Flick is not expected to receive a replacement in the current transfer window.

The report states that Barcelona have no intention of signing another midfielder despite Frenkie de Jong’s injury, with the club continuing to trust the existing options already available in the squad.

At the same time, Barcelona’s sporting department is maintaining its plan regarding Marc Casado, with the midfielder still expected to leave the club on loan or in a permanent transfer if the right opportunity arrives.

title: Fake kits worth £18m seized by UK police over safety concerns
description: Counterfeit football shirts worth over £18m were seized in the UK during the course of the World Cup because of safety concerns, with the Fire Service warning the outfits can be a danger to those who wear them.
fullText:
Counterfeit football shirts worth over £18m were seized in the UK during the course of the World Cup because of safety concerns.

The tournament produced a spike in the sales of fake shirts and kits, but the Fire Service has warned that these items are far more flammable and dangerous than the genuine articles.

More than 220,000 pieces of football merchandise were recovered by the Police Intellectual Property Office as part of Operation Bloxwich, a UK police enforcement effort targeting counterfeit goods.

One raid involved 1,618 fake strips being taken from a warehouse in Birmingham, including replicas of star players such as France's Kylian Mbappe and Norway's Erling Haaland.

Detective Sergeant Jamie Kirk, who took part in the operation, told BBC Sport: "I understand why people want to buy these shirts, people love football and the incentive is the lower price - but these shirts, they're not regulated and could be highly flammable.

"They have dyes in them which could be harmful to skin, particularly children's skin. While the cost is lower - and that's the attractive part - potential damages to you and the people making them is just far too high."

Cheshire Fire and Rescue Service tested the flammability of counterfeit shirts compared with genuine kit and found the fake kits were much more flammable.

"It's quite clear that the counterfeit one melted as soon as the burner went on it," said watch manager Dave Jackson, who carried out the test by hanging them two at a time on an iron bar and used blow torches to set them alight. "I really had to try to make the genuine one ignite and melt."

One of the factors driving the sales of counterfeit kits is their low price. Several websites sell them for as little as £12, while the cost of some official Premier League strips start at £85.

"It's extremely dangerous. It's something that can cause horrific injuries to your children. It's just not worth the savings," Jackson added.

Kate Caffrey, head of intelligence at the Intellectual Property Office, warned that fake shirts are not regulated.

"Counterfeit strips are not subject to health and safety regulations, British Standards [or] EU standards," she told BBC Sport.

"We've got evidence that counterfeit goods are made in appalling conditions, so you just don't know what's in them, how they're being made and there's no way of providing any safety reassurances.

"Counterfeiting is criminal, these are manufactured and supplied by criminal networks, criminal gangs and they're very adept at targeting markets where there's demand. So the World Cup, the forthcoming Premier League season - they're going to flood the market with fake shirts.

"The price of a fake shirt, you don't know what you're funding with that money."

title: Soccer legend moving on from broadcasting following World Cup: ‘Farewell to the studio’
description: Former Swedish soccer star Zlatan Ibrahimovic is moving on from broadcasting after making his debut with FOX Sports for the World Cup.
fullText:
Swedish soccer legend Zlatan Ibrahimovic, who joined FOX Sports as an analyst for the World Cup, is moving on from broadcasting.

Ibrahimovic announced following Sunday’s final between Spain and Argentina that the 2026 World Cup will be his one and only event as a broadcaster.

“I’ve been talking a lot the last one and a half months, so these will be my last words,” Ibrahimovic said on air. “It has been an absolutely pleasure to share this studio with you guys. You made it very easy for me. I want to thank also FOX studio, I want to thank America, I want to thank also all the other people you don’t see. … You made it easy for me.

“I hope America, you enjoyed it as much as I did. And this is also my farewell to the studio. This was the first and last time for me, so thank you everybody.”

Ibrahimovic drew widespread praise throughout the World Cup for his work as an analyst.

Andrew Marchand, who covers sports media for The Athletic, described him as “a World Cup TV star.”

Still, Sweden’s all-time leading goal scorer says he is moving on from broadcasting.

In addition to playing for the Swedish national team, Ibrahimovic also played for AC Milan, FC Barcelona, Inter Milan, Juventus, Manchester United, Paris Saint-Germain and other professional teams during his career.

He scored 62 goals with the Swedish national team and is considered to be one of the best goal scorers in soccer history.

title: Thousands welcome home Argentina squad, without Messi, despite World Cup loss
description: Thousands of fans braved the cold and rain Monday to give a rapturous welcome to the Argentine football squad who landed back home without star Lionel Messi, after their World Cup final loss to Spain."Today it's hard to appreciate what we achieved, but this squad reached two consecutive World Cup finals."
fullText:
Thousands of fans braved the cold and rain Monday to give a rapturous welcome to the Argentine football squad who landed back home without star Lionel Messi, after their World Cup final loss to Spain.

It was not the homecoming Argentines had hoped for, four years after millions celebrated their team's tournament victory in 2022, but fresh scenes of joyful chaos filled the street as they danced, sang and set off fireworks to thank the players.

The Argentine Football Association (AFA) said earlier that some squad members would not be on the flight, and iconic team captain Messi was not seen descending from the aircraft in images broadcast on local television.

Coach Lionel Scaloni and the rest of the team were welcomed with a red carpet outside the aircraft and a military band.

They then boarded a bus, waving to frenzied fans along the way.

Despite falling short of a second straight championship, "Argentines will celebrate anyway," said housewife Marga Ledezma. The 36-year-old thanked Messi for "all the years he made us happy," urging him not to quit playing for Argentina, but admitting this would be difficult as he was 39.

Argentina lost 1-0 to Spain in a game that has been criticized in international media for a plethora of fouls and displays of poor sportsmanship from the Albiceleste.

"I think they criticize us because we know what it's like to suffer, that we get up, we fall down again, and we keep going," said Ledezma.

Many fans highlighted that the real victory for Argentina was the 2-1 semi-final win over England, played against the backdrop of a lingering dispute between the countries over the sovereignty of the Falkland Islands.

Some carried flags declaring "The Falklands are Argentine." FIFA is investigating the Argentine team after players held up a banner bearing the phrase after their win against England which sparked a backlash from London.

"This team united us all, and we should be grateful for that. We will always be there for them, rain or shine. I would say thank you to Messi and that he gave it his all," said Leonardo Barrientos, 36, who works in a textile workshop.

Maria Ortiz, 28, came to welcome the team with her two-year-old, and said Spain "deserved the win" but she was "happy for what the team gave us."

Messi wrote on Instagram on Monday that "the pain is immense and it's going to take a long time for this wound to heal."

"I'll also hold on to all the good memories," he wrote alongside a photo of himself. "Today it's hard to appreciate what we achieved, but this squad reached two consecutive World Cup finals."

With the tournament over, President Javier Milei announced that a future date agreed with the team would be set as a public holiday to celebrate their performance in the World Cup. No further details have been announced.

While crestfallen after the loss, thousands of Argentines headed to the traditional celebration point at the Obelisk in Buenos Aires where they partied for hours on Sunday evening.

Late at night, brief clashes broke out at the monument and police used water cannons, tear gas, and rubber bullets to disperse revellers. Fifteen people were detained, according to police sources.

title: Jorge Jesus confirms talks with CBF and digs at Ancelotti call-up
description: After being eliminated from the FIFA 2026 World Cup in the round of 16, with a 1-0 defeat to Spain, the Portugal national team announced the hiring of coach Jorge Jesus on July 10.The former Flamengo ...
fullText:
After being eliminated from the FIFA 2026 World Cup in the round of 16, with a 1-0 defeat to Spain, the Portugal national team announced the hiring of coach Jorge Jesus on July 10.

The former Flamengo manager arrived to take over the team in place of the dismissed Roberto Martínez.

This Monday (20), Jorge Jesus confirmed that he held talks last year to take charge of the Brazilian national team, after the 4-1 defeat to Argentina that led to Dorival Júnior’s departure.

"I ended up not going to coach the Brazilian national team, but I had already been given indications to make Brazil’s preliminary squad list for the match that would be played in March after they had lost 4-1 to Argentina," JJ told Portugal’s Canal 11.

In fact, after the defeat to their South American rivals in the qualifiers, the national team’s next commitment came during the FIFA window in June 2025, when Carlo Ancelotti made his debut in charge of Brazil.

Jorge Jesus even took a swipe at the Italian coach’s squad selection for the 2026 World Cup.

According to the new Portugal coach, Pedro, whom he worked with at Flamengo, is the country’s best "striker" (center forward) and deserved a place among those called up.

"What would I have done? Brazilian players are all over the world. Besides that, there is a lot of quality in the Brazilian Championship... And would I have called up a different squad from what Ancelotti did? No, of course I would have with one or two players. I think that right now the best striker is Pedro, and that is a little biased because he was my player at Flamengo. One to three different players, but I agree with most of what he did. But the way of looking at the game and training is the difference."

This article was translated into English by Artificial Intelligence. You can read the original version in 🇧🇷 here.

title: Stay or go - at 28, Rashford faces big Man Utd call
description: The World Cup is over, and Marcus Rashford returns home not knowing if his future at club level lies with Manchester United.
fullText:
Marcus Rashford's World Cup ended with a considered and thoughtful social media post, issued in the aftermath of England's amazing 6-4 third-place play-off victory over France in Miami.

Disappointments are guaranteed in football, he said, before opening out in a more positive vein and stating firmly: "Keep believing in us. Our time will come."

He added: "Now it's time for me to rest, reflect, spend some quality time with family and friends, and come back a better version of myself."

'Reflect' is the big word in that overview.

At 28, Rashford is moving into what should be the prime years of his career.

He has just won his first league title, La Liga with Barcelona, has 77 England caps and featured in six out of eight games as Thomas Tuchel's side delivered the country's best World Cup performance since 1966.

But in reality, a large part of what Rashford will be reflecting on with his family - some of whom are also key figures in terms of guiding his career - is whether his club future lies at Manchester United.

Rashford is contracted to Manchester United until 2028. His salary of £325,000 a week makes him the club's top earner at a time when minority owner Sir Jim Ratcliffe is trying to stay on top of salary costs, which are, by some distance, the club's highest expenditure.

Barcelona had an option, in the loan deal they agreed for Rashford last season, to turn it into a permanent transfer for £26m.

As expected, that deadline passed in the middle of June without it being activated.

There was another clause in Rashford's contract that allowed other clubs - Liverpool and Manchester City excepted - to buy him for £40m, which expired earlier in July. As expected, no-one triggered that either.

Sources close to the player felt it was illogical to think a club would come in and offer more than Barcelona would have had to pay.

With England's World Cup campaign at an end, Rashford is now entitled to a three-week break.

Unless the timings are altered, or his situation changes, he will return to Manchester United just as they begin a six-day training camp in the Republic of Ireland, scheduled around an historic meeting with old rivals Leeds at Croke Park on 12 August.

Suggestions around Rashford have been that he is relaxed about the prospect of returning to training at United.

For a start, there is not the same confrontational relationship between the player and his coach.

Ruben Amorim fell out with the forward within weeks of his arrival from Sporting Lisbon. Amorim eventually exiled him, which triggered a loan move to Aston Villa.

Then the head coach put Rashford in his 'bomb squad', along with Alejandro Garnacho, Antony, Tyrell Malacia and Jadon Sancho, when pre-season training began last summer.

The other four players have all now left the club.

Amorim's replacement, Michael Carrick, is a former team-mate of Rashford's. He has also been his coach and was, briefly, his manager, following Ole Gunnar Solskjaer's dismissal in 2021. The pair are said to get on well.

Carrick's only public statement around Rashford, towards the end of last season, was conciliatory in nature, which is in keeping with his general personality.

He is so far yet to be asked what his stance is around the player returning to the fold.

For their part, United are sticking to their refusal to renegotiate a deal with Barcelona on reduced terms or consider a second loan to the Catalan giants.

It is not clear what interest there is in Rashford at the Nou Camp given Barca have already signed England wide-man Anthony Gordon and are about to bring in Germany forward Karim Adeyemi from Borussia Dortmund.

The consistency of United's messaging is being articulated in a summer where they are keen to prove they will not be bullied into overpaying to get deals over the line.

That financial discipline may have consequences - while the feeling was Elliot Anderson would have joined Manchester City anyway, United did not countenance paying the £116m Nottingham Forest eventually sold him for.

The same is true of Mateus Fernandes and his £85m switch from West Ham to Tottenham.

Nevertheless, Manchester United's enhanced used of data through director Michael Sansoni and his dedicated team means the club feel bullish about their chances of landing alternative targets who offer better value.

Clearly just as United need active sellers to buy from, they need buyers so they can offload those players they do not want.

In Rashford's case, there are a limited number of clubs globally who could match his current salary. And where a club are interested, the player has to want to join them.

Judging by his recent social media post, he would not do anything to jeopardise his England place.

BBC Sport has also been told of some Premier League clubs he would not be keen to move to.

So where will Rashford be when the transfer window closes on 1 September? Currently, the likeliest answer, by some distance, is Manchester United.

After that: Any Spanish club, any Turkish club, any German club. The vagueness there underlines the current lack of market for the 15th highest scorer in United's history, with 138 goals - someone who is only 17 short of a place in the top 10.

There are plenty, including former captain Gary Neville, who feel Rashford's time at Old Trafford is over and there should be no reconciliation.

In the end though, it may have to happen - and not everyone would be disappointed.

"A fit and focused Marcus Rashford has the ability to be one of the best players," said Rashford's friend Sam Smith - the Wrexham forward who scored the winner in their pre-season victory over Carrick's team in Helsinki on Saturday - when speaking to the United We Stand podcast.

"He has had a difficult time at United and getting away from it all was a good thing.

"I don't know what is going to happen but as his friend, I hope he comes back and proves how good a player he was, how good he can be and starts scoring goals again."

title: World Cup final watched by 15.8m people on BBC
description: Spain's victory in Sunday's World Cup final attracts a peak audience of 15.8 million on BBC One and BBC iPlayer.
fullText:
Spain's victory over Argentina in Sunday's World Cup final attracted a peak audience of 15.8 million on BBC One and BBC iPlayer.

The BBC enjoyed a 64% share of all TV viewing for the match, with an average of 13.9 million watching as Ferran Torres' extra-time strike sealed Spain's second World Cup triumph.

The final was streamed 11.1 million times on BBC iPlayer and the BBC Sport website and app, including 2.4m UHD streams on iPlayer.

The BBC Sport live text coverage on the website and app was viewed more than 18.8 million times, including 14.2 million views in the UK, providing fans with the latest news, analysis and updates from the match.

Saturday's third-place play-off between England and France brought in a peak audience of 11.8 million on BBC One and iPlayer, with an average audience of 10.8 million.

Across the World Cup as a whole, the BBC saw an overall reach of 42.8 million across TV viewing.

Audiences streamed World Cup matches 237 million times on BBC iPlayer and the BBC Sport website and app, more than double the 2022 World Cup, and football dominated BBC iPlayer, with World Cup content accounting for 33% of everything streamed during the competition.

Additionally, the BBC Sport website and app attracted 57.2 million unique visitors, with fans viewing BBC Sport content 1.7 billion times, match highlights of World Cup matches were streamed 67.3 million times while the new BBC Football YouTube channel generated 100 million views across a diverse range of content.

Across social media, clips, interviews, analysis, press conferences and fan moments attracted 2.55 billion video views.

The top-performing video of the World Cup was Egypt winger Mostafa Zico's interview after Egypt went out to Argentina, with 77 million views on TikTok alone.

Alex Kay-Jelski, Director of BBC Sport, said: "This has been a World Cup we'll never forget, and that's entirely down to the millions of people who chose to experience it with the BBC.

"Thank you to everyone who watched, streamed, listened, read, shared and joined us throughout the tournament.

"Whether you were watching at home, following on your phone during the working day, catching up on Football Daily or diving into our new interactive experiences, you made this a summer of football that brought the nation together."

title: Messi says 'pain is immense' after losing World Cup final
description: Lionel Messi says "the pain is immense" and that it "will take time to heal" after Argentina lost 1-0 to Spain in the World Cup final.
fullText:
Lionel Messi says "the pain is immense" and that it "will take time to heal" after Argentina lost 1-0 to Spain in the World Cup final.

Ferran Torres' extra-time strike saw Spain crowned world champions for a second time, ending Argentina's defence of the title they won in 2022.

Messi has now lost two World Cup finals after Argentina were defeated 1-0 by Germany in 2014.

At 39 years old, Messi is unlikely to feature at the 2030 tournament, though he is yet to clarify his international future.

"The pain is immense, and this wound will take time to heal. But I also hold onto all the good things," Messi wrote on social media.

"The matches we turned around by giving it our all - moments that will remain in our memories forever - and the support of an entire country which, combined with this group's hard work and effort, brought us back among the world's elite once again.

"It is hard to fully appreciate our achievement right now, but this group reached two consecutive World Cup finals.

"Thank you from the bottom of my heart for every greeting and message. Once again, we managed to unite as a country and stand together, sharing the immense pride of being Argentine.

"I also want to congratulate Spain on winning the championship."

Messi contributed eight goals and four assists as Argentina reached the final for the third time in the past four World Cups.

He played a key role in his side coming from behind to beat Egypt and England in the knockout stages, but could not overcome a Spain side that conceded one goal in the whole tournament.

Argentina midfielder Enzo Fernandez, who was shown a second yellow card in second half injury time for a late challenge on Pau Cubarsi, wrote on social media that he and his team-mates had "defended these colors with pride, humility, and commitment".

Fifa's disciplinary committee has appointed a disciplinary and ethics prosecutor to investigate altercations between both sets of players at the full-time whistle.

"For years, this group has represented us in the best possible way. It teaches that competing isn't just about winning; it's about giving your all for the jersey and never giving up," Fernandez added.

title: Three winners and three losers for Barcelona from the 2026 FIFA World Cup
description: Sixteen Barcelona players crossed the Atlantic with different expectations and so many dreams. Eight are now set to return as world champions.However, beneath all those medals, the 2026 FIFA World Cup...
fullText:
Sixteen Barcelona players crossed the Atlantic with different expectations and so many dreams. Eight are now set to return as world champions.

However, beneath all those medals, the 2026 FIFA World Cup altered reputations, saw some stocks rise, some fall and left several Blaugrana stars wondering what might have been.

There are goals that win matches, goals that define tournaments and, every once in a while, goals that rewrite the way an entire career will be remembered.

Ferran Torres now has one of those. A substitute on the biggest night of his life, the forward became the man who returned Spain to the summit of world football and added the second star to their jersey.

It was a fitting conclusion to a World Cup with a distinct Barça flavour.

Here are Barcelona’s three biggest winners and three players who leave the World Cup with considerably less than they hoped for.

Spain’s hero. (Photo by Buda Mendes/Getty Images)

For much of his Barcelona career, Ferran Torres has lived in football’s grey areas. Winger or centre-forward? Starter or substitute? Important, certainly, but indispensable? Not always.

Those debates will continue when club football returns. Ferran is even linked with a move to French giants PSG at the moment. However, some moments sit above such conversations.

Ferran entered the World Cup final from the bench and, in the second period of extra time, provided the breakthrough Spain spent two hours waiting for.

Argentina had frustrated La Roja, survived wave after wave of pressure and forced the match into its most anxious stage. Then Ferran arrived to have his Iniesta moment.

The finish made Spain world champions for the second time. It also made Ferran immortal.

Regardless of whether he stays at Barça, moves to PSG or whatever else happens in his career going forward, he has etched his name in history with one swing of his left foot.

For Ferran, that is the ultimate victory.

Two World Cups, zero appearances. (Photo by Lars Baron/Getty Images)

There is no pleasure in placing Ronald Araujo on this side of the list. His World Cup was not lost through a mistake, a bad performance or a tactical demotion. His body let him down.

Araujo suffered a muscle tear in training before Uruguay’s opening match and did not feature as Marcelo Bielsa’s team exited in the group stage.

Four years earlier, he had travelled to Qatar while recovering from injury and did not play there either. Two World Cups have now passed without one of Uruguay’s finest defenders stepping onto the pitch.

At his best, Araujo possesses qualities almost no centre-back can replicate: recovery speed, physical dominance, aggression and the capacity to survive in vast spaces.

He has always been important for the national team irrespective of his club form, but his availability has always been a question mark.

While Cubarsi leaves North America with the Best Young Player award and a starting role in a world-champion defence, Araujo leaves without a minute played.

At a time when his club career seems to be going nowhere, this does not make for good reading from the defender’s perspective.

The Best Young Player of the tournament. (Photo by Buda Mendes/Getty Images)

Pau Cubarsi went to the World Cup as one of the finest young defenders in football. He returns as one of the finest defenders in the sport. That may be his greatest achievement of all.

Cubarsi started the final alongside Aymeric Laporte at the heart of a Spain defence that conceded just once throughout the tournament.

There is little theatre to Cubarsi’s football. There is no need for a desperate recovery tackle when he has already read the danger.

There is no constant desire to make defending look dramatic. The teenager had a stupendous World Cup both with and without the ball.

It was a World Cup full of 29-year-old performances from the 19-year-old starlet.

Against Argentina, while Lionel Messi attempted to find the spaces from which he had tormented opponents for an entire generation, Cubarsi was right next to him to stop him from doing that.

The Spaniard had a World Cup to remember, made everyone sit up and take notice, and returns to Barcelona with his reputation enhanced manifold.

Plagued by injuries again. (Photo by Al Bello/Getty Images)

Raphinha’s tournament disappeared more quietly. He started Brazil’s opening draw against Morocco and began their second group match against Haiti before injury struck after around 40 minutes.

Raphinha’s disappointment is about opportunity. World Cups come once every four years, and this was supposed to be a tournament played in the heart of his prime.

A stage on which one of Barcelona’s senior attacking figures could carry his club form into Brazil colours and add another layer to his career. Injuries did not allow him to enjoy that arc.

At the top of the world at 19. (Photo by Carl Recine/Getty Images)

Perhaps the strangest measure of Lamine Yamal’s greatness is that he can become a world champion at 19 and leave behind the feeling that there is still another level to come.

His tournament did not begin in the best possible fashion. Lamine arrived after an April hamstring injury and was initially managed carefully, coming from the bench in Spain’s opening draw against Cape Verde.

On his first start, against Saudi Arabia, he needed only 10 minutes to score and help transform a Spanish attack that had looked pedestrian without him.

Later, he was named player of the match in the 3-0 knockout victory over Austria without even registering a goal or assist.

Yet Lamine did not need to defeat Messi single-handedly for the tournament to belong on his rapidly expanding list of achievements.

The final itself was subdued, for him and almost everyone else, before Spain’s substitutes changed its course. However, he stayed part of the structure that carried La Roja all the way.

European champion as a teenager. World champion at 19. Football usually asks young players to wait their turn. Lamine has turned that into a joke.

A peripheral role for Gavi with Spain. (Photo by Dan Mullan/Getty Images)

Calling a 21-year-old world champion a loser should come with an obvious disclaimer.

Gavi has just won football’s greatest prize. Nothing about that should be diminished. However, individual tournaments can contain uncomfortable truths even beneath gold medals.

Gavi started Spain’s opening match against Cape Verde and played 71 minutes, but dropped out of the XI when Alex Baena returned against Saudi Arabia.

He later appeared from the bench against Austria, yet when Spain reached the final, Luis de la Fuente’s preferred starting midfield consisted of Rodri, Fabian Ruiz and Dani Olmo.

For a player whose rise once felt unstoppable, he has now moved to the periphery. It is a shame that his 2026 World Cup will be remembered for that one poor performance against Cape Verde.

He is still young enough and talented enough to turn it around, and the Barcelona midfielder will be keen to have a more prominent role for Spain in tournaments going forward.

Spain’s medal will hang around his neck forever, but a competitor like Gavi would have wanted to earn it more than he did.

title: Three winners and three losers for Real Madrid from the 2026 FIFA World Cup
description: The recently concluded FIFA World Cup was a special one for Real Madrid as the club’s players broke innumerable records and completed the campaign by handing Los Blancos the position as the club wit...
fullText:
The recently concluded FIFA World Cup was a special one for Real Madrid as the club’s players broke innumerable records and completed the campaign by handing Los Blancos the position as the club with the highest-ever goal contributions in a single edition.

Several of the team’s big names showed up in grand style at the event and delivered what was expected from them and more. Others, however, produced disappointing campaigns that are better forgotten and moved on from.

Beyond the existing first-team players, there were also four of the club’s new signings on display at the event – all of whom experienced varying campaigns.

The tournament officially closed last night with Spain proclaimed the champions of the world, and the timing could not be better to review the performances of the many Real Madrid stars who were in action.

Madrid Universal brings you the three winners (hits) and three losers (flops) from Real Madrid in the FIFA World Cup 2026.

The Golden Boot winner. (Photo by Buda Mendes/Getty Images)

The French talisman once again proved why the FIFA World Cup is his event to shine. On the stage where the best players come under pressure and struggle to deliver, Mbappe was again in the limelight week in and week out for all the right reasons.

Mbappe finished the tournament as the Golden Boot winner, scoring ten goals and winning it by a margin of two goals over Lionel Messi. He became the first player to score double-digit goals at a World Cup since Gerd Muller in 1970.

Beyond that, the player also became the all-time leading goalscorer in the competition in what comes as a massive accomplishment. He even became the French national team’s top-scorer of all time.

Watching Mbappe play with complete precision in the final third, needless to say, will hand Mourinho ideas of how to maximise his output. The Frenchman once again proved he is a lethal weapon in clutch moments, and will look to produce the same for Real Madrid.

Failed to deliver on the expectations. (Photo by Alex Grimm/Getty Images)

Turkey entered the World Cup making their maiden appearance after 24 years, but there was a sense of excitement around the team given the young talents who have lit up world football in recent years.

Arda Guler was the centrepiece of Turkey’s plans and was even seen as the team’s marquee player heading into the event. A big brunt of their eventual group stage elimination, thus, unsurprisingly fell on him.

Across his team’s three group stage games, Guler managed to score only one goal and failed to rack up a single assist. Considering that he played all 270 minutes possible, that was underwhelming.

He struggled to establish any form of rhythm in midfield for his side and saw his creative output plummet shockingly. Guler even struggled to keep up with the physicality of the game, especially against Paraguay.

Bronze medal and Bronze boot for Bellingham. (Photo by Buda Mendes/Getty Images)

Bellingham was another Real Madrid player who dominated the goal-scoring charts, albeit as a less expected candidate. There were shouts to drop the player from the squad ahead of the event, and he shut his critics in the best manner possible.

The Englishman finished as his country’s top goalscorer at the event with seven goals, even ahead of Harry Kane. He was third in the Golden Boot race after only Mbappe and Messi.

What made his contributions special, however, was the weight each of his goals carried and the situations they arrived in.

After all, almost all of his goals were either winners or equalisers that came under the highest of pressure, and both his braces arrived in knockouts.

Bellingham’s braces against Mexico and Norway, in particular, were very special as they single-handedly drove England to the semifinals of the competition in games where they did not look as dominant as expected.

What his campaign proved what they he is best deployed in the attacking third with a free-floating role – something he did not enjoy last season at Real Madrid. Mourinho’s focus, thus, will be to recreate that environment around him at the club level as well.

Poor. (Photo by Florencia Tan Jun/Getty Images)

Portugal were one of the pre-tournament favourites, and Bernardo Silva one of the team’s guaranteed starters heading into the tournament. His transfer to Real Madrid, thus, generated much excitement and he was expected to be a key player.

The narrative, however, was shocking as Silva suffered a fall from grace at the worst possible time. He fell down the manager’s pecking order and lost his starting status immediately after Portugal’s opener.

Across four appearances, the midfielder had no goals, no assists and just one shot attempted. He only played 94 minutes throughout the tournament.

For a player of Bernardo Silva’s stature to see a fall in prominence to the extent that unfolded, needless to say, makes him a flop of the event. For it to arrive just before his signing for Real Madrid only makes matters worse.

World champion. (Photo by Carl Recine/Getty Images)

A lot is being said about Spain’s defence in the World Cup, especially given that they conceded just one goal throughout the tournament. Marc Cucurella, Real Madrid’s new left-back signing, was the team’s starting left-back and an integral part of that line.

His individual campaign may not have stellar numbers, but Cucurella was integral defensively in all of Spain’s knockout clashes.

Further, the left-back pushed high in the final third to add width to the team in Nico Williams’ absence and did a flawless job in balancing his duties at both ends of the field.

Until the final, Cucurella was not dribbled past in any of Spain’s knockout clashes – a statistic that shows his defensive prowess. He ended the tournament with two assists and seven clean sheets, numbers that any defender would be proud of.

Further, he made a strong case to be Mourinho’s starting player at left-back.

Valverde was unable to lead by example. (Photo by Lars Baron/Getty Images)

As the captain and focal point of Marcelo Bielsa’s Uruguay, Valverde was the player who faced the most backlash after their shocking group stage elimination.

His side failed to win a single game in the competition and concluded the group stages with two draws and a defeat, thereby becoming the highest-ranked nation to crash out in the group stage.

Valverde, on an individual level, did not do much to improve his team’s situation either and was as poor as the rest of his compatriots. He finished with no goals and no assists in 237 minutes and failed to hit the target in six out of seven attempted shots.

Neither did Valverde retain control in midfield for Uruguay, nor did he take up any creative responsibilities. He has been known to come up in clutch moments over the years but failed to deliver even once for his nation.

Heading back to the Spanish capital, the Real Madrid captain will undoubtedly look to begin on a fresh slate and come back stronger.

title: Lionel Messi breaks silence after ‘immense pain’ of World Cup final loss
description: Messi has surely played his final ever World Cup game after missing out on back-to-back titles against Spain
fullText:
Lionel Messi has broken his silence on the “immense pain” he is feeling after Argentina’s World Cup final defeat to Spain.

Messi was visibly distraught after missing out on back-to-back titles in what will surely be his final World Cup appearance, with the Argentinian hero turning 39 this tournament.

His side failed to lose with much dignity, with a mass brawl breaking out at full-time as Leandro Paredes threw punches at the celebrating Spanish players.

But Messi has now taken to Instagram to post an emotional statement, less than 24 hours out from that gut-wrenching failure in New Jersey.

"The pain is immense, and it will take time for this wound to heal. But I also hold on to all the good," he said.

"The matches we turned around, giving our all, matches that will forever remain in our memories, the support of an entire country that, together with the hard work and effort of this group, led us to be, once again, among the best in the world.

"Today it's hard to fully appreciate what we accomplished, but this group reached two consecutive World Cup finals.

"Thank you from the bottom of my heart for every greeting and every message. Once again, we managed to unite as a country and be together, sharing the immense pride of being Argentinian.

"I also want to congratulate Spain on winning the championship."

Messi did not confirm whether he would retire from international football after Argentina manager Lionel Scaloni cast doubt on the forward’s future.

“He is 39 years old, it is unbelievable,” Scaloni said in a tearful press conference. “It was always crystal clear to me that he would play until he chose to stop and that everyone would support him.

“I hope everyone is proud of him, because he is the best football player ever to set foot on a pitch.”

Messi scored eight goals and made four assists in a fantastic campaign for Argentina.

The 1-0 defeat to Spain, courtesy of an extra-time strike from Ferran Torres, was the only match he failed to contribute a goal in.

title: Messi says 'pain is immense' after losing World Cup final
description: Lionel Messi says "the pain is immense" and that it "will take time to heal" after Argentina lost 1-0 to Spain in the World Cup final.
fullText:
Lionel Messi says "the pain is immense" and that it "will take time to heal" after Argentina lost 1-0 to Spain in the World Cup final.

Ferran Torres' extra-time strike saw Spain crowned world champions for a second time, ending Argentina's defence of the title they won in 2022.

Messi has now lost two World Cup finals after Argentina were defeated 1-0 by Germany in 2014.

At 39 years old, Messi is unlikely to feature at the 2030 tournament, though he is yet to clarify his international future.

"The pain is immense, and this wound will take time to heal. But I also hold onto all the good things," Messi wrote on social media.

"The matches we turned around by giving it our all - moments that will remain in our memories forever - and the support of an entire country which, combined with this group's hard work and effort, brought us back among the world's elite once again.

"It is hard to fully appreciate our achievement right now, but this group reached two consecutive World Cup finals.

"Thank you from the bottom of my heart for every greeting and message. Once again, we managed to unite as a country and stand together, sharing the immense pride of being Argentine.

"I also want to congratulate Spain on winning the championship."

Messi contributed eight goals and four assists as Argentina reached the final for the third time in the past four World Cups.

He played a key role in his side coming from behind to beat Egypt and England in the knockout stages, but could not overcome a Spain side that conceded one goal in the whole tournament.

title: Uefa president boycotts World Cup final in protest against Fifa
description: Uefa president Aleksander Ceferin did not attend the World Cup final between Argentina and Spain in protest against Fifa.
fullText:
Uefa president Aleksander Ceferin did not attend the World Cup final between Argentina and Spain in protest against Fifa.

While the Slovenian did travel to Mexico City for the opening game between the co-hosts and South Africa, he snubbed the trip to New Jersey on Sunday after a series of decisions taken by world football's governing body.

Uefa reacted strongly after United States striker Folarin Balogun escaped a suspension following an intervention from US President Donald Trump.

This was a key factor in Ceferin's decision not to go to the final.

Uefa had criticised Fifa's decision at the time, calling it "unprecedented, incomprehensible and unjustifiable".

"When the certainty of rules is no longer guaranteed by its guardians, the integrity of the game is at stake and the credibility of a competition is undermined," Uefa said in a statement.

Ceferin is also firmly against many other changes driven by Fifa president Gianni Infantino, including half-time shows. They will not be introduced to the Champions League or the European Championship.

Pop giants Madonna, BTS, Shakira and Justin Bieber were among the acts to perform in the event, which extended the usual 15-minute half-time break to 27 minutes 22 seconds.

Uefa is also strongly against a 64-team World Cup, which Infantino appeared to open the door to during the tournament.

After referee Omar Artan was denied entry to the US before the tournament started, Uefa invited the Somali to officiate the Super Cup between Paris St-Germain and Aston Villa on 12 August.

The high ticket prices have also proved controversial within Uefa.

Uefa will broadly freeze ticket prices for Euro 2028 - meaning supporters will be able to buy five for the price of a single parking space at a World Cup stadium in the United States this summer.

BBC Sport reported on Friday how Uefa has instructed its VARs not to consider diving under the new mistaken identity protocol, as Fifa has during the World Cup. This led to Switzerland striker Breel Embolo being sent off in the quarter-final defeat to Argentina.

Players who cover their mouths in confrontations with opponents will not be shown red cards in Uefa competitions either.

The issue of players covering their mouths became high profile in February when Benfica winger Gianluca Prestianni raised his shirt while speaking to Real Madrid's Vinicius Jr during a Champions League game.

The Argentina international was accused of racist abuse and provisionally banned for one match. Following a Uefa investigation, Prestianni was found guilty of homophobic conduct and banned for six matches - three of which were suspended.

The law change for a red card was the brainchild of Fifa president Infantino, who wanted something which would have "a deterrent effect" at the World Cup.

Paraguay's Miguel Almiron was the first player to be sent off in his side's World Cup group game against Turkey. Arsenal defender Piero Hincapie then saw red in Ecuador's last-16 exit to co-hosts Mexico.

title: What did England learn during ODI series win over India?
description: After beating India 2-1, BBC Sport asks if England are finally building momentum one year out from the World Cup.
fullText:
What a difference three weeks makes.

At the end of June, a dark cloud hung over the England camp, with Test captain Ben Stokes retiring after a 2-1 series defeat by New Zealand.

That campaign brought an end to coach Brendon McCullum's time in charge of the red-ball side, and plenty of questions remain about the future of the Test team, but the New Zealander has enjoyed an upturn in fortunes in July, with his limited-overs outfit beating India 4-0 in a T20 series and 2-1 in one-day internationals.

Defeating the world number one side in both formats, and overtaking them at the top of the rankings in the 20-over game, is no mean feat and England may feel they are now beginning to show the sort of momentum they last displayed in the build-up to their World Cup victory in 2019.

With the next edition of the tournament a little over a year away, BBC Sport takes a look at whether England's 50-over team is finally tracking in the right direction.

England's only significant change in the India series was a new opening combination of Ben Duckett and Jacob Bethell, and it bore fruit at Lord's.

The left-handed duo became England's sixth opening pair since the 2023 World Cup, with Phil Salt, Jamie Smith and Zak Crawley currently out of contention and Will Jacks and Rehan Ahmed considerations for the middle order after spells at the top.

They had a decent start in the first ODI at Edgbaston, putting on 63, but Duckett was dismissed first ball in the second fixture at Cardiff, with Bethell following shortly afterwards as England found themselves 8-2.

In London, however, the pair flogged a tired India attack, taking advantage of benign batting conditions to put on the highest ODI opening stand at Lord's, with Duckett pressing on for his fourth century in the format.

Bethell, who has not previously batted higher than four for England in white-ball cricket, started his ODI opening career with innings of 14 and four, but cut a composed figure at the Home of Cricket.

He played in a typically positive fashion, striking at 97.85 as he plundered 11 fours and two sixes before falling for 91, just short of a second ODI ton.

The 22-year-old's promotion up the order means England can carry an extra bowler, with the hosts fielding three seamers and spinner Adil Rashid alongside all-rounders Sam Curran and Will Jacks in the latter two matches of this series.

If England are to regain the World Cup, having surrendered it meekly in 2023, much of the responsibility will rest on Joe Root's shoulders.

The 35-year-old struggled by his standards in the one-day format for a number of years following the 2019 victory, averaging just 29 between 2020 and 2023, and his record saw him left out of the ODI set-up for the entirety of 2024.

However since his recall, Root has enjoyed a remarkable uptick in form, averaging 81.5 from 21 innings with four centuries.

He has now made six consecutive scores over 50, stretching back to the start of the series in Sri Lanka in January, becoming the first England batter to achieve that feat.

Root's unbeaten 76 at Edgbaston kept England in contention, with a 121-run partnership with Liam Dawson coming between collapses of 6-107 and 4-30, while an undefeated 99 in Cardiff anchored England's successful chase.

Coming in after the record-breaking opening stand on Sunday, Root enjoyed a rare opportunity to cut loose, with his strike-rate of 154.17 the highest of any ODI score over 50 in his ODI career.

If there is a question to be answered about England's side, it is who will fill the seamer slots in the XI.

With the World Cup to be played on the hard decks of South Africa and Zimbabwe, England will likely persist with three outright fast bowlers with the support of Curran.

Spearhead Jofra Archer is an automatic selection, assuming he remains fit, while Gus Atkinson did his cause no harm in his two outings against India, taking 3-50 in Cardiff and keeping to an economy rate of four at Lord's when no other bowler was under six.

The third slot remains up for grabs, with neither Josh Tongue nor Saqib Mahmood consolidating their place in the side.

Tongue took one wicket in two matches and was expensive at Lord's, conceding 43 runs from five overs, while Mahmood took 2-52 in Cardiff but was rotated out for Tongue for the series finale.

Brydon Carse returned to the squad for this series having recovered from wrist and elbow injuries that have kept him out of international cricket, while the selectors have also favoured the all-round skills of Jamie Overton and may return to him once he has recovered from the thigh injury he sustained in the Indian Premier League.

With a five-match Ashes series preceding the World Cup, England may expect at least one seamer to go down with injury, and will want a strong group of pacers ready to step up to the tournament squad.

There is also slight concern about spinner Adil Rashid, who has had a quiet 2026, taking eight wickets in ODIs and only one in this series.

The 38-year-old's economy rate for the year is consistent with recent efforts, remaining under six, while he can still claim to be England's biggest threat in overs 11-40 - taking 32 of their 72 wickets in that period since the start of last year.

England will return to the one-day format at the tail-end of the summer against Sri Lanka, with matches at Chester-le-Street, Headingley and The Oval between 22-27 September.

Their next taste of international cricket will be in the format as they play three matches in Australia in mid-November, before they sample World Cup conditions with three fixtures in South Africa in mid-January.

There is set to be an ODI series against Pakistan in the 2027 summer before the World Cup starts on 4 October.

title: Thank you for making this a World Cup to remember on the BBC
description: BBC Director of Sport Alex Kay-Jelski's message as BBC Sport delivers a record-breaking World Cup across TV, radio, online and social.
fullText:
As an incredible World Cup comes to an end, everyone here at the BBC wanted to say thank you.

Thank you to the 42.8 million people who tuned in to our live match coverage on television or streamed games on iPlayer, the BBC Sport app and website 237 million times. That's a lot of football.

Whether you were one of the 24 million who watched England v Argentina in the semi-final, the late-night 9.1 million who stayed up until 4am for England v Mexico, the 2.4 million who saw Scotland beat Haiti in the middle of the night, or part of the hardcore crew of half a million who gave Iran v New Zealand a chance at 2am and were rewarded with a four-goal thriller.

Thank you to those of you who trusted us to not spoil your mornings, coming to iPlayer or the BBC Football YouTube channel every day for our eight-minute highlight packages. There were so many of you that we saw the biggest ever day and week in iPlayer's history.

Thank you to the 57.2 million unique browsers who used our app and website 1.7 billion times, watching clips of our games, interviews and journalism, following our amazing live pages and reading stories from our journalists covering everything from the Trump-Fifa-Balogun scandal to tactical trends and the US entry denial of Somalian referee Omar Artan.

World Cup final watched by 15.8m people on BBC

And to those of you who spent ages deep in our new 3D tool, watching the game as if you were Lionel Messi, Cristiano Ronaldo or Erling Haaland charging down the goalkeeper - it was used almost five million times. We love trying new things and you using them helps us decide what to do next.

Thank you to the millions of you on the move tuning in to the games on Radio 5 Live or BBC Sounds, realising there are few better ways to sit on the edge of your seat than listening to John Murray, Ian Dennis and the team. And to those who helped the visualised Football Daily podcast reach more people than ever before.

As for those of you whose fingers are tired from six weeks of swiping more than two billion times through our social and digital content, thank you for devouring our behind-the-scenes footage of Harry Kane's hilarious high-pitch interview, our attempts to introduce Olivier Giroud to Irn-Bru, our YouTube tactical dissections and, of course, Wayne Rooney, Joe Hart and Micah Richards' rowing trip down the Hudson.

Thank you to all the new people using the BBC, buying licence fees from QR codes or finding our app, socials or shows for the first time. It's great to have you with us.

There's lots more we have to offer if you stick around too, whether that is downloading our app with its swipeable video feed, following your favourite sport or team for a personalised experience, or checking out our range of documentaries and podcasts across iPlayer and Sounds.

It doesn't have to be sport either - there are amazing dramas, comedies, music, children's shows and news coverage across the BBC, to mention just a few things.

Mostly, however, you spent time with the BBC during the World Cup - and 94% of you in the country did - thank you for choosing us and trusting us.

We loved bringing all the action, fun and drama to you.
