title: Real Madrid new signing Cucurella gets emotional after Spain win World Cup: ‘It’s not easy to get here’
description: Real Madrid new signing Marc Cucurella was one of Spain’s standout performers during the 2026 FIFA World Cup, playing a key role in the team’s triumph with consistently impressive displays at left...
fullText:
Real Madrid new signing Marc Cucurella was one of Spain’s standout performers during the 2026 FIFA World Cup, playing a key role in the team’s triumph with consistently impressive displays at left-back.

Following Spain’s victory over Argentina in the final, during which he excelled once again, attention quickly turned to a promise the defender had made before the tournament: if La Roja won the World Cup, he would get a tattoo of head coach Luis de la Fuente.

Speaking to the media after the celebrations in New Jersey, Cucurella confirmed that he intends to keep his promise.

“I don’t know, I have to think about where to get it. People will ask me, it has to be somewhere visible. We have a long trip ahead and they’ll give me ideas. I’ll have to do it,” Cucurella said with a laugh, as quoted by Mundo Deportivo.

The new Real Madrid defender defender laughed as he admitted there was no way out of the bet now that Spain had become world champions.

Cucurella was key to Spain’s World Cup triumph. (Photo by Carl Recine/Getty Images)

Reflecting on the achievement, Cucurella became emotional as he spoke about the challenges he faced throughout his career.

“It’s not easy to get here. It’s tough during your teenage years; you have to make decisions. Sometimes there are people who tell you you’re not good enough and that you won’t make it,” Cucurella said, almost tearing up.

“To all those people, I’d like to thank them for taking the time to give me their advice. Also to everyone who has helped me – we’ve both been stubborn. Today we’ve been rewarded for being here. It hasn’t been an easy summer for us, so we’re really happy.”

Talking about the team’s performance Cucurella felt Spain deserved to lift the trophy after controlling the final against the defending champions.

“We already knew what to expect. They tried to play their own style of football, with a lot of intensity. The team was fully focused, didn’t get drawn into a mind game, and we deserved the victory,” said Cucurella.

“We were playing against the reigning champions and we dominated the match,” he emphasised.

The left-back also praised Spain’s evolution throughout the tournament.

“It’s incredible. From the very first day we arrived, we tried to play our own style. We started off a bit slowly, but we kept improving and ended up playing some incredible football. We dominated France and Argentina and didn’t concede a single goal.”

Spain finished the tournament having conceded just one goal, setting a new defensive benchmark for a World Cup-winning side.

World champion. (Photo by Justin Setterfield/Getty Images)

For Cucurella, that achievement reflected the collective effort of the entire squad, as he said:

“When you don’t concede, it’s much easier to win. We’ve all been doing an incredible job. Take the way Oyarzabal pressed, for example. It’s a team effort. We attack and defend together. That’s the key.”

Cucurella then acknowledged that they were still coming to terms with being champions

“We haven’t had much time. We’re looking forward to getting back to Spain, where there are lots of people who haven’t been able to come, and we’re keen to celebrate with all our fans. We’d like to thank them for their support; we’ve felt right at home.”

Before leaving for the airport, Cucurella also praised Rodri, who has been constantly linked with Real Madrid, and won the Golden Ball at the World Cup.

“He’s very important; sometimes it’s not about scoring or providing assists. He controls everything, organises the team – when we press, when we attack… he’s very important,” Cucurella said.

“Whenever we’re under pressure, he gives us the chance to play through him. He’s had an incredible tournament and deserves the award.”

title: Stay or go - at 28, Rashford faces big Man Utd call
description: The World Cup is over, and Marcus Rashford returns home not knowing if his future at club level lies with Manchester United.
fullText:
Marcus Rashford's World Cup ended with a considered and thoughtful social media post, issued in the aftermath of England's amazing 6-4 third-place play-off victory over France in Miami.

Disappointments are guaranteed in football, he said, before opening out in a more positive vein and stating firmly: "Keep believing in us. Our time will come."

He added: "Now it's time for me to rest, reflect, spend some quality time with family and friends, and come back a better version of myself."

'Reflect' is the big word in that overview.

At 28, Rashford is moving into what should be the prime years of his career.

He has just won his first league title, La Liga with Barcelona, has 77 England caps and featured in six out of eight games as Thomas Tuchel's side delivered the country's best World Cup performance since 1966.

But in reality, a large part of what Rashford will be reflecting on with his family - some of whom are also key figures in terms of guiding his career - is whether his club future lies at Manchester United.

Rashford is contracted to Manchester United until 2028. His salary of £325,000 a week makes him the club's top earner at a time when minority owner Sir Jim Ratcliffe is trying to stay on top of salary costs, which are, by some distance, the club's highest expenditure.

Barcelona had an option, in the loan deal they agreed for Rashford last season, to turn it into a permanent transfer for £26m.

As expected, that deadline passed in the middle of June without it being activated.

There was another clause in Rashford's contract that allowed other clubs - Liverpool and Manchester City excepted - to buy him for £40m, which expired earlier in July. As expected, no-one triggered that either.

Sources close to the player felt it was illogical to think a club would come in and offer more than Barcelona would have had to pay.

With England's World Cup campaign at an end, Rashford is now entitled to a three-week break.

Unless the timings are altered, or his situation changes, he will return to Manchester United just as they begin a six-day training camp in the Republic of Ireland, scheduled around an historic meeting with old rivals Leeds at Croke Park on 12 August.

Suggestions around Rashford have been that he is relaxed about the prospect of returning to training at United.

For a start, there is not the same confrontational relationship between the player and his coach.

Ruben Amorim fell out with the forward within weeks of his arrival from Sporting Lisbon. Amorim eventually exiled him, which triggered a loan move to Aston Villa.

Then the head coach put Rashford in his 'bomb squad', along with Alejandro Garnacho, Antony, Tyrell Malacia and Jadon Sancho, when pre-season training began last summer.

The other four players have all now left the club.

Amorim's replacement, Michael Carrick, is a former team-mate of Rashford's. He has also been his coach and was, briefly, his manager, following Ole Gunnar Solskjaer's dismissal in 2021. The pair are said to get on well.

Carrick's only public statement around Rashford, towards the end of last season, was conciliatory in nature, which is in keeping with his general personality.

He is so far yet to be asked what his stance is around the player returning to the fold.

For their part, United are sticking to their refusal to renegotiate a deal with Barcelona on reduced terms or consider a second loan to the Catalan giants.

It is not clear what interest there is in Rashford at the Nou Camp given Barca have already signed England wide-man Anthony Gordon and are about to bring in Germany forward Karim Adeyemi from Borussia Dortmund.

The consistency of United's messaging is being articulated in a summer where they are keen to prove they will not be bullied into overpaying to get deals over the line.

That financial discipline may have consequences - while the feeling was Elliot Anderson would have joined Manchester City anyway, United did not countenance paying the £116m Nottingham Forest eventually sold him for.

The same is true of Mateus Fernandes and his £85m switch from West Ham to Tottenham.

Nevertheless, Manchester United's enhanced used of data through director Michael Sansoni and his dedicated team means the club feel bullish about their chances of landing alternative targets who offer better value.

Clearly just as United need active sellers to buy from, they need buyers so they can offload those players they do not want.

In Rashford's case, there are a limited number of clubs globally who could match his current salary. And where a club are interested, the player has to want to join them.

Judging by his recent social media post, he would not do anything to jeopardise his England place.

BBC Sport has also been told of some Premier League clubs he would not be keen to move to.

So where will Rashford be when the transfer window closes on 1 September? Currently, the likeliest answer, by some distance, is Manchester United.

After that: Any Spanish club, any Turkish club, any German club. The vagueness there underlines the current lack of market for the 15th highest scorer in United's history, with 138 goals - someone who is only 17 short of a place in the top 10.

There are plenty, including former captain Gary Neville, who feel Rashford's time at Old Trafford is over and there should be no reconciliation.

In the end though, it may have to happen - and not everyone would be disappointed.

"A fit and focused Marcus Rashford has the ability to be one of the best players," said Rashford's friend Sam Smith - the Wrexham forward who scored the winner in their pre-season victory over Carrick's team in Helsinki on Saturday - when speaking to the United We Stand podcast.

"He has had a difficult time at United and getting away from it all was a good thing.

"I don't know what is going to happen but as his friend, I hope he comes back and proves how good a player he was, how good he can be and starts scoring goals again."

title: Spain Hero Ferran Torres Career, Girlfriend, & Family: 5 Fast Facts You Need to Know
description: Ferran Torres became Spain's World Cup hero by scoring the only goal in a 1-0 victory over Argentina in the 2026 final. The post Spain Hero Ferran Torres Career, Girlfriend, & Family: 5 Fast Facts You Need to Know appeared first on HEAVY.
fullText:
Ferran Torres became Spain’s World Cup hero after scoring the only goal in a 1-0 victory over Argentina in the 2026 FIFA World Cup final. The Barcelona forward broke the scoreless tie in the 106th minute of extra time at MetLife Stadium, delivering Spain its second World Cup championship. The strike was Ferran’s first goal of the soccer tournament and the third World Cup goal of his international career. Here’s what you need to know.

Spain and Argentina remained scoreless through the first 90 minutes and the opening period of extra time before Ferran finally broke through.

The forward scored in the 106th minute, giving Spain the 1-0 advantage it protected through the final whistle. His goal secured the 2026 World Cup title and became the biggest moment of his international career.

Ferran entered the final without a goal in the tournament, but he delivered when Spain needed him most. The winner raised his career total to three World Cup goals and added another major trophy to a résumé that already included Spain’s Euro 2024 championship.

Ferran was born in Foios, Valencia, on February 29, 2000. Because he was born on Leap Day, his birthday only appears on the calendar once every four years.

A lifelong Valencia supporter, Ferran joined the club’s youth academy when he was 6 years old. He made his senior debut against Eibar on December 16, 2017, becoming the first player born in 2000 to appear in La Liga.

Ferran moved to Manchester City in 2020 and won the Premier League and EFL Cup during the 2020-21 season. Barcelona signed him in January 2022, bringing him back to Spain.

Despite struggling at times during his first two seasons, Ferran helped Barcelona win a domestic treble in 2024-25 before enjoying the best scoring campaign of his career the following season with 21 goals as the club captured another La Liga title.

IT'S FERRAN TORRES IN THE FIFA WORLD CUP FINAL 🇪🇸

THE ENTIRE SPAIN BENCH GOES TO CELEBRATE WITH HIM! pic.twitter.com/RVRXvnyOXG

— FOX Sports (@FOXSports) July 19, 2026

Ferran normally plays as a striker for Barcelona, but he has also been effective on both wings.

Spain used him on the right side during parts of the World Cup while Lamine Yamal recovered from injury. His versatility gave Spain another dangerous attacking option throughout the tournament.

Ferran entered the World Cup in outstanding form after finishing as Barcelona’s joint-leading scorer in La Liga with 16 goals. He also scored against Iraq and assisted against Peru during Spain’s final warm-up matches before the tournament.

His ability to play anywhere across the front line has helped him total 84 goals and 17 assists in 358 senior appearances for club and country.

Ferran was raised by his mother, M. Dolores Garcia Alcover, and grew up alongside his older sister, Arantxa.

Their relationship remains one of the closest in his life. While playing for Manchester City, the siblings even decided to get matching tattoos to celebrate their bond.

“We’ve always been so close, always together, in the good moments and in the bad ones, and this is why we decided to both have the same tattoo, because we are very important to each other,” Ferran said.

Ferran has kept much of his family life private and has rarely spoken publicly about his father.

Lamine Yamal, Ferran Torres, Dani Olmo, Pedri González, Pau Cubarsí, Eric Garcia, Joan Garcia… WORLD CHAMPIONS!

📸 @fifaworldcup pic.twitter.com/Nt6tktf2WA

— FC Barcelona (@FCBarcelona) July 19, 2026

Between 2021 and 2023, Ferran was in a relationship with professional equestrian Sira Martínez, the daughter of former Spain manager Luis Enrique, according to Yahoo Sports.

Sira competes in show jumping and is a member of the Barcelona Royal Polo Club. The couple began dating while Ferran played for Manchester City and stayed together after his transfer to Barcelona.

According to multiple reports, they ended their relationship in 2023 as Sira focused on her equestrian career. Based on Ferran’s public social media activity, he is currently believed to be single.

Away from soccer, Ferran has often cited former Spain striker David Villa as his childhood idol, while former Valencia star Gaizka Mendieta was the name on his first Valencia jersey.

title: Donald Trump wants World Cup back in U.S. 'immediately'
description: Donald Trump wants the World Cup in the U.S. again while still in office, but he will have to wait awhile for a return.
fullText:
The United States just finished hosting the 2026 World Cup for the first time in more than 30 years, and even though it will be a while before it does it again, President Donald Trump will be requesting to host it once more "immediately."

Trump made the comments to Fox Sports' Jenny Taft ahead of the World Cup final between Spain and Argentina on Sunday, July 19, lauding the success of the tournament that saw millions of people flock to the U.S., Canada and Mexico, and take part in a widely successful event.

He admitted to FIFA president Gianni Infantino that he was skeptical of how well the World Cup would do in the U.S., but the great turnout and reception resulted in him wanting it once more.

"I had a great idea for Gianni. I said, ‘You have to do two countries.’ So announce us again next time, and announce another country after that, that’ll take out some of the anger and the shock," Trump said. "But based on the numbers, we will be requesting it again immediately. But I said to take out the shock, you could announce us and maybe then for the next one another, and even another. But we have to do this again, and we have to do it while I’m around. You hear that, Gianni?”

If Trump was referring to the World Cup being in the U.S. again while he is in office, it won't happen since his term ends in 2028. The 2030 World Cup will be hosted by Morocco, Spain and Portugal, while the 2034 edition will be in Saudi Arabia.

The next available World Cup is in 2038, which the U.S. could very likely host since Oceania and North America will be eligible to host under the current cycle rules. However, that's in 12 years.

Trump has made his presence within FIFA known since 2018, when the U.S. was awarded the hosting duties for the 2026 tournament. He has become a close friend with Infantino, who presented Trump with the FIFA Peace Prize in December in a controversial move. He also inserted himself into the red card conversation surrounding U.S. star Folarin Balogun, when he pushed Infantino to reverse the decision to suspend him for one match, which ended up happening in another decision surrounded by controversy.

The president was inside MetLife Stadium for the final between Spain and Argentina, and was on the field to present the medals and trophy for Spain's title, which included him awkwardly still on stage as La Roja celebrated holding the trophy.

This article originally appeared on USA TODAY: Donald Trump wants World Cup back in U.S. 'immediately'

title: From Super Bowl to World Cup: The major sporting events Trump has attended in his 2nd term
description: President Donald Trump has prioritized attending huge sporting events during his second term.
fullText:
President Donald Trump has prioritized attending huge sporting events during his second term.

On Sunday, Trump will become the first U.S. president to attend a men’s World Cup final when Argentina and Spain face off in one of the year’s most anticipated sporting events. His appearance marks just the latest sporting event he will attend since returning to office in January 2025.

Here’s the list of other major sporting events he has attended since returning to office.

Trump became the first sitting U.S. president to attend a Super Bowl during on Feb. 9, 2025 in New Orleans. The game came about a month after a New Year’s Day terrorist attack in the city killed 14 people and injured dozens more. Trump met with families of the victims before the game.

Trump left the game about 10 minutes into the second half—which left pundits speculating that he left early because his desired team, the Kansas City Chiefs, was losing.

Trump, who is close with UFC President Dana White, has attended multiple fights during his second term in office. He was met with cheers when he attended a fight at the Kaseya Center in Miami alongside Secretary of State Marco Rubio in April 2025. He later attended a UFC match with Rubio at the Prudential Center in Newark, New Jersey in June 2025.

Trump reaffirmed his love for the UFC after he recently hosted a fight on the South Lawn of the White House on June 14 to celebrate America’s 250th anniversary.

Trump attended the 2025 Daytona 500 just weeks after going to Super Bowl LIX. He had also attended the popular race in 2020 during his first term in office. His appearance was marked by his motorcade taking a lap around the Daytona International Speedway.

Trump’s appearance at the World Cup final on Sunday is not the first time he attended a FIFA event. Last year, Trump was mocked after he awkwardly joined in on Chelsea FC’s FIFA Club World Cup celebration.

Chelsea winger Cole Palmer later said he did not realize Trump would be on the stage as his team lifted up their trophy.

“I knew he was going to be there, but I didn’t know he was going [to] be on the stand where we lift the trophy. So, I was a bit confused,” said Palmer, via USA TODAY.

Trump faced widespread criticism after his attendance at the U.S. Open men’s tennis final snarled security lines, which delayed fans getting into the USTA Billie Jean King National Tennis Center in September 2025.

The 2025 match between world No. 1 Jannik Sinner and No. 2 Carlos Alcaraz started about 30 minutes late as a result of enhanced security measures. Trump also faced backlash after it was reported that US Open officials sent an email to broadcasters asking them to censor any negative reactions to his presence.

Trump made waves after fans blamed the president for “cursing” the New York Knicks with his attendance at Game 3 of the NBA Finals at Madison Square Garden.

The NYPD and the Secret Service cancelled the popular watch party outside the world’s most famous arena, leaving die-hard fans outraged. Fans were also prohibited from bringing bags inside the arena, and were told to expect enhanced security measures before entering the game.

Trump was also met with boos as he was shown on screen before tip-off.

The Knicks ended up breaking their 13-game winning streak in Game 3, making it their only loss in the best-of-seven matchup with the San Antonio Spurs.

title: Leandro Paredes sent off after final whistle as Argentina’s World Cup ends with punches and in disgrace
description: Paredes was involved in an ugly clash with Spain’s Eric Garcia and Gavi following the full-time whistle
fullText:
Argentina’s World Cup ended with punches thrown and the former champions left in disgrace following a 1-0 defeat to Spain in the final.

There were chaotic scenes after the full-time whistle in New Jersey as the Spain substitutes ran onto the pitch to celebrate victory.

Nahuel Molina, the Argentina defender, appeared to swing a punch into the stomach of Spain captain Rodri as he ran past him onto the field.

Then, there was a violent clash involving midfielder Leandro Paredes, who was pictured shoving Spain’s Eric Garcia by the throat.

Paredes wrestled Spain’s Gavi to the ground and pushed into his face while holding him by his substitute bib.

Spain’s Mikel Oyarzabal and Rodri sprinted past Molina after the final whistle was blown and La Roja were crowned champions.

Molina appeared to swing at Rodri, leading to the Spain captain stopping in his tracks and approaching the Argentina defender while shouting.

Molina then squared up to Rodri and bumped him in the chest, which was the flashpoint that triggered Spain’s Eric Garcia to step in.

Paredes, who was booked during the final and was fortunate to not receive a second yellow card, then flew towards Garcia. He jabbed him in the throat just as Argentina coach Lionel Scaloni approached, wagging his finger and trying to break up the scuffles.

With Scaloni separating Garcia and other Argentina coaching staff members getting involved, Paredes then turned his attention to Spain’s 21-year-old midfielder Gavi - who by that point was already wrestling with Argentina’s Thiago Almada.

Paredes, 32, spun Gavi to the ground and appeared to push into his face while holding him by the bib. Several of Gavi’s Spanish team-mates then stepped in to shield him, with Spain’s Borja Iglesias crashing into Paredes.

It was a sorry way for Argentina’s tournament to end after they had taken an emotional path to the final. They were attempting to a second World Cup in a row for captain Lionel Messi, who was playing on the World Cup stage for the final time.

But the antics of the rest of the Argentina team were quickly condemned as the scenes after the final whistle soured Spain’s deserved victory, with former England goalkeeper Joe Hart labelling Argentina’s behaviour “disgusting”.

"It's a disgrace,” Gary Neville said on ITV. “I love the competitiveness of the Argentines, fighting and scrapping for their lives, Messi has propped them up, but it’s been a disgrace the last few games.”

“Paredes threw two or three haymakers into someone's face. He went for them,” Alan Shearer said on BBC. “There is no place or space for that.

“We know how much it means to them and we know how much it hurts to lose, but there is a way to lose. Too many times we have seen that from Argentina. The reaction after the final whistle is terrible.”

BBC pundit Hart said: “There was one man on the pitch who did have class and that was Lionel Messi, who shook hands with every one of those Spanish players.“The game’s done. They could have absolutely no complaints about what happened today. Disgusting behaviour.”

Hart added: “Spain fully deserve it. They have been absolutely superb all tournament.“They have dominated all game against a rigid, rugged Argentina. The best team has won this World Cup.”

Argentina were previously criticised for their aggressive behaviour during their 2-1 semi-final win over England, after which players unveiled a banner claiming sovereignty of the Falklands Islands.

Fellow pundit Wayne Rooney said of Argentina’s behaviour: “Does it surprise you? It doesn’t surprise me.“We’ve seen this with Argentina before. It’s just not the reaction you want. We’ve seen some of the reaction after knocking England out in the last round.“That’s just sad. If you lose a game of football, be graceful and get out of there. It’s really poor from them.”

ITV’s co-commentators were in agreement that Spain were deserved winners, with Lee Dixon accusing Argentina of “anti-football” and questioning how Paredes had remained on the pitch, while Ally McCoist said Spain were “the only team trying to win this game”.

Meanwhile, the tears rolled down Lionel Messi’s face as he watched the Argentina fans following defeat to Spain.

Messi, 39, is yet to confirm his future plans but the expectation is that one of the greatest to grace the game has played his final World Cup before retirement.

The Argentina captain was left frustrated as Spain dominated the final, with the European champions winning 1-0 thanks to Ferran Torres’ goal after extra time.

Messi had inspired a series of brilliant comebacks in the knockout stages to help Argentina to a second successive final but Spain were deserved winners.

Messi scored eight goals in an incredible tournament and the Argentina fans chanted his name as Spain’s Rodri collected the player of tournament award.

Messi then led his team-mates to collect their runners-up medals. He then stood and cried while he watched the Argentina fans serenade him.

The record eight-time Ballon d’Or winner will be 43 at the next men’s World Cup, which takes place in Spain, Portugal and Morocco - but will kick off in Argentina.

Messi waited until Spain lifted the World Cup, but then quickly left the pitch in what was perhaps a bitter end to his World Cup journey.

Elsewhere, Argentina centre-back Cristian Romero snubbed Donald Trump’s handshake during the presentation of the runners-up medals.

Romero started the match at the heart of the Argentine defence, alongside Lisandro Martinez, but was subbed off at the 70-minute mark.

However, the 28-year-old Tottenham defender still made his mark after that as he snubbed a handshake from Trump when the US president was conducting the medal presentation.

Trump was handing out medals to both teams, as well the individual tournament awards, and Romero shook the hand of both Fifa president Gianni Infantino and Mexican president Claudia Sheinbaum, who were either side of Trump, but did not engage with the US president.

title: Is Trump the first president to attend a World Cup final?
description: President Donald Trump will be attending the final men’s World Cup game in East Rutherford, New Jersey on Sunday afternoon.
fullText:
President Donald Trump will be attending the final men’s World Cup game in East Rutherford, New Jersey on Sunday afternoon.

Argentina and Spain are set to face off for the World Cup final at 3 p.m. ET in East Rutherford, New Jersey after a month of thrilling games that have gripped sports fans across the globe. The White House confirmed last week that Trump will be attending Sunday’s highly anticipated game as the U.S. caps off hosting one of the largest sporting events in the world.

“His attendance will cap what has been the most watched, most secure, and most successful World Cup in American history,” White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt told reporters. “This is a fitting conclusion to a tournament that showcased America’s ability to host the world on the grandest stage.”

FIFA president Gianni Infantino, a close ally of the president, also confirmed last month on “Fox & Friends” that he and Trump will jointly present the trophy to the winners of the World Cup.

“We will be together with the president enjoying the final and handing the trophy to the winner, of course, together,” Infantino said.

Trump faced criticism earlier this month after it was reported that he called Infantino asking FIFA to overturn the suspension of U.S. men’s national team striker Folarin Balogun. Balogun was ultimately allowed to play, but the U.S. men’s national team still lost to Belgium.

The appearance will mark the first time a U.S. president will attend the men’s World Cup final. The U.S. has hosted the tournament only once before, under then-President Bill Clinton.

When the U.S. last hosted the men’s World Cup in 1994, Clinton delivered remarks at the opening ceremony but did not attend the final match between Brazil and Italy. Instead, then-Vice President Al Gore attended the final game in Pasadena, California and handed the trophy to Brazil’s team captain.

But Clinton did attend the women’s final game in Pasadena when the U.S. women’s soccer team clinched their second World Cup title in 1999.

title: 'The last of Messi': Argentines mourn loss and end of an era
description: Nervous excitement and rowdy cheers turned into anguished groans, nailbiting tension and tears, as Argentines' hope of yet another epic comeback led by Lionel Messi in the World Cup fizzled out against Spain.Empty because of Messi, because I won't see him in a World Cup again.
fullText:
Nervous excitement and rowdy cheers turned into anguished groans, nailbiting tension and tears, as Argentines' hope of yet another epic comeback led by Lionel Messi in the World Cup fizzled out against Spain.

At the Cafe Relax bar in the historic center of Buenos Aires, fans with tears in their eyes after the final whistle blew on the 1-0 defeat said that what saddened them most was that it was almost certainly 39-year-old Messi's last shot at a World Cup.

"I feel empty. Empty because of Messi, because I won't see him in a World Cup again. This is the last one," said Camilo Cichero, a 22-year-old architecture student who was comforting his crying friend.

"It's not how we expected it to go. At least it gives me peace of mind knowing he's already won everything there was to win."

Composing himself, his friend Juan Saenz, 22, a law student said: "This was the last of Messi ...it was the very end, and it finished this way."

"I was born while Messi was playing, and this was the end of that era."

Around the city fireworks went off, car horns honked, and thousands of fans still made their way to the iconic Obelisk, the traditional place for football celebrations.

Initially crestfallen, they soon began partying again to celebrate their team and many said that despite the defeat, they would take to the streets to welcome the team home on Monday.

"Thank you so much, players!!! You fought until the very end. Argentina will always stand tall," President Javier Milei wrote on X.

After a scrappy World Cup run built on a series of narrow victories, Argentines were confident they could pull off a second victory in a row in the final in New Jersey.

Draped in their blue-and-white flag and donning the national football jersey, they braved an icy cold day to pack bars and public squares, fizzing with excitement at the start of the game.

In Cafe Relax, which displays a flag saying it is "forbidden to forget" the Falkland Islands, which Argentina invaded in 1982 only to be ejected by a British task force, nervous energy and excited chants gave way to tension by half-time.

In the second half angry shouts at the screen were interspersed with excited chants, and increasingly foul language, while some sat arms folded, feet tapping and chewing their nails or burying their heads in the flag.

"Oh no, it can't be, please, please!" wailed one woman when Enzo Fernandez was sent off for two yellow cards.

Before extra time began, Teresa Dolores del Rio Varela, 87, complained that "it drags on and on, and you start to get nervous. I'm happy, but I just want it to end because it's getting long."

Joaquin Romero, 22, who works in sales said the game was "incredibly complicated. I don't know how it'll happen, but it's going to happen for us. We're Argentine. We're champions and we're going to win."

A dramatic extra-time goal by Ferran Torres cast a pall of silence over the bar for the last agonizing minutes.

Cichero said he was sad for Messi but dismayed at the game.

"I didn't like how Argentina played. I didn't like the game plan, the substitutions, the players. I didn't like the referee."

Abel Torres, a 62-year-old retiree wearing a blue-and-white jester hat and blowing a vuvuzela throughout the game, teared up as he described the "incredibly emotional" game.

"Argentina gave it their all. Today was a true example of what Argentina is all about: that despite the hard times and the obstacles we face, we always rise again."

In Messi's hometown Rosario, hundreds gathered to celebrate the player and his team with fireworks and chants.

"We've already won a cup. We can't just break down in tears -- we have to celebrate. Messi has already put us at the very top of the world," Sergio Irrazabal told AFP.

title: Spain and UNICEF stole the show: Winners, losers of World Cup final
description: Spain won its second men's World Cup when it beat Argentina in extra time in the final. Here are the winners and losers of the full day.
fullText:
After 104 matches across 16 cities in three countries, Spain reigns supreme in the 2026 World Cup.

La Roja won the tournament by beating Argentina 1-0 in the final on Sunday, July 19 at MetLife Stadium in East Rutherford, N.J. This is their first title since 2010.

Lionel Messi and Argentina were neutralized and did not have a shot on goal in the entire match, which went into extra time.

After both teams won their groups and then clawed their way through the knockout rounds, a charity photo from 2007 of a young Messi giving a baby Lamine Yamal a bath went viral as a wink of destiny.

Here are the winners and losers of the World Cup final between Spain and Argentina:

If you would have told football fans that Spain was going to win the World Cup after their scoreless opening draw with newcomer Cape Verde, they would have laughed. But La Roja gutted out the tournament as one of the most well-rounded sides playing. Besides winning their second men's World Cup, which is their first since 2010, La Roja nearly ran the table with the player awards, too. AND they are the first country to hold both the men's and women's World Cup titles at the same ... darn ... time.

The Argentina goalkeeper had his work cut out for him as Spain dominated in possession. He broke the World Cup final record with 11 saves in the match, including diving to swat away Lamine Yamal's free kick in second half stoppage time. Even though Argentina lost, Martínez can hold his head high.

Cape Verde is truly the people's champion of the 2026 World Cup. The African nation made its debut in the tournament this summer and rattled Spain in the opening match. They then had Argentina's back against the ropes in the round of 32. Knowing that they gave tough fights to the eventual runner-up and champion, the Blue Sharks have to feel good.

That photo of a young Lionel Messi bathing a baby Lamine Yamal was absolutely EVERYWHERE leading up to the World Cup final. It is actually from a charity photo shoot that UNICEF put together back in 2007. The non-profit was already somewhat of a household name, but this embeds them deep into sports history in a way that no publicist could ever plan.

Argentina's late surge did not come against a Spain team that controlled the entire World Cup final. And, after a tournament riddled with controversy, the side didn't even show a glimpse of greatness in the match. Instead, their highlight reel will feature Enzo Fernández's second yellow card in the second half, which equaled a red card. He was ejected and Argentina was forced to continue with 10 players. They finished the fixture with absolutely ZERO shots on goal, far from a performance that they will want to remember.

President Donald Trump was in attendance at the World Cup final along with members of his family and other dignitaries. He has attempted to position himself as an influential decision-maker in the tournament, including taking credit for having Folarin Balogun's red card rescinded. The president was booed when he came on the pitch to award the medals and trophy to Spain and then made a fool of himself by sticking around too long.

We will always love "Yellow," but the Coldplay frontman let us down with the extremely chaotic World Cup halftime show, which he was supposedly in charge of curating. FIFA was clearly gunning for the Super Bowl with the performance, the first of its kind at the global tournament. But where the NFL is thoughtful in getting a single, or sometimes a few, popular stars to deliver an artful performance, World Cup organizers provided a disjointed, throbbing mish mash that was simply hard to watch.

title: John Strong mistakes Matt Damon for Brad Pitt on Fox World Cup final broadcast
description: Fox play-by-play announcer John Strong had a celebrity mix-up during Sunday afternoon’s broadcast of the 2026 FIFA World Cup final between Spain and Argentina at MetLife Stadium in East Rutherford, New Jersey. In the first half, the Fox broadcast showed actor Matt Damon in attendance. But Strong thought it was another high-profile actor, Brad Pitt.…
fullText:
Fox play-by-play announcer John Strong had a celebrity mix-up during Sunday afternoon’s broadcast of the 2026 FIFA World Cup final between Spain and Argentina at MetLife Stadium in East Rutherford, New Jersey.

In the first half, the Fox broadcast showed actor Matt Damon in attendance. But Strong thought it was another high-profile actor, Brad Pitt.

“Brad Pitt also among the fans here,” Strong said on the Fox broadcast, alongside color commentator Stu Holden.

“Oh, it’s Matt Damon,” Strong realized. “That’s Matt Damon.”

Strong at least realized the gaffe quickly and apologized.

“Apologize to both,” Strong said.

The timing of this mix-up makes it even funnier, with Damon starring as Odysseus in Christopher Nolan’s The Odyssey (which hit theaters on Friday), after Pitt starred in the blockbuster film Troy in 2004.

Damon was at the World Cup final with his wife, Luciana Barroso, who is from Argentina.

Odysseus pulled up to the World Cup final

Matt Damon’s wife, Luciana, is from Argentina

— Yahoo Sports (@YahooSports) July 19, 2026

Unfortunately for them, the Lionel Messi-led Argentina squad wasn’t able to repeat as World Cup champions and lost 1-0 to Spain.

title: Spain win World Cup after Messi's Argentina fall flat
description: Spain won their second World Cup on Sunday, collecting the trophy from US President Donald Trump after Ferran Torres' extra-time strike broke the stubborn resistance of Lionel Messi's Argentina.Argentina coach Lionel Scaloni admitted Spain had deserved to win.
fullText:
Spain won their second World Cup on Sunday, collecting the trophy from US President Donald Trump after Ferran Torres' extra-time strike broke the stubborn resistance of Lionel Messi's Argentina.

More than 80,000 people crammed into the MetLife Stadium in East Rutherford, New Jersey, to witness a tense, tight final punctuated by cynical Argentine fouls that ended 1-0.

The defending champions struggled to compete with dominant Spain and were left with a mountain to climb when Enzo Fernandez was sent off in the 93rd minute for a second yellow card after a reckless challenge on defender Pau Cubarsi.

Argentina barely had an attempt on goal in the entire match but held on grimly, with goalkeeper Emiliano Martinez making a series of saves.

The 2010 winners, who had peppered their opponents' goal, finally broke the deadlock early in the second half of extra-time.

The goal came from a brilliant knock-back by Nico Williams for fellow substitute Torres, who smashed the ball high into the net in the 106th minute.

At the end, the Spain players cavorted on the pitch while Messi was in tears as he trudged up to collect his runners-up medal.

The ceremony was presided over by Trump, who was loudly booed as he walked onto the pitch at the end of the game with Gianni Infantino, the chief of world football's governing body FIFA.

Trump had to be asked by Infantino to move to the side as he lingered on stage while the Spanish team held the trophy aloft.

The 80-year-old even did a quick version of his trademark "Trump dance".

An emotional Spain coach Luis de la Fuente said his players were a "source of pride" for the nation.

Spain captain Rodri, who won the player of the tournament award, said it was hard to put his feelings into words, 14 months after he returned from a serious knee injury.

"I think now we're like in a cloud," he said. "It's been a very tough time. I just want the new generations to see my example as an opportunity -- when you go down you can rise again."

Messi, 39, may have played his last World Cup game and he can take enormous credit for dragging his ageing team to the final after they were pushed to the limit in knockout games against Egypt and England.

But on Sunday, he was largely shut out of the game as Spain imposed their smooth passing game and refused to shirk physical challenges.

The South Americans had been aiming to become the first team since Brazil in 1962 to win consecutive World Cups. But Spain were faster, slicker and younger.

Argentina coach Lionel Scaloni admitted Spain had deserved to win.

"They were the better side -- that's the truth -- but I'll cherish the memory of (this team), of what they achieved, and of the value of making it this far," said Scaloni, who later broke down in tears at his post-game press conference.

"We have to place immense value on this because it takes so much effort," he added.

Musical headliners Madonna, Shakira, Justin Bieber, BTS and Coldplay were joined by Brazilian soccer icons Ronaldinho and Ronaldo and "Ted Lasso" actors in the first-ever World Cup final half-time show.

The star-packed, Super Bowl-style extravaganza also featured a tribute to late football icon Pele, and a rendition of "Seven Nation Army" led by Gustavo Dudamel and featuring musicians from the US, Venezuela and Iran.

On Saturday, England beat France 6-4 in a high-scoring thriller to seal third place -- their best finish since they won the tournament in 1966.

Kylian Mbappe became the competition's all-time leading scorer with 22 goals.

The France forward also won the Golden Boot with 10 goals, two ahead of Messi, finishing as top scorer for the second World Cup in a row.

The build-up to the World Cup in Canada, Mexico and the United States was dominated by fears over sky-high prices, geopolitical tensions and concerns that brutal summer heat would make matches unplayable.

But the biggest, most complex edition of football's showpiece ever staged -- expanded from 32 teams to 48 -- will be remembered for thrilling football and a feel-good factor.

Trump has hailed the tournament as "the most successful sporting event, maybe in the history of the world".
